title: Lyndon Johnson

infobox.value: Lyndon Baines Johnson (1908-08-27)August 27, 1908  [Gillespie County, Texas](/wiki/Gillespie_County,_Texas "Gillespie County, Texas"), U.S.
[Johnson Family Cemetery](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson_National_Historical_Park "Lyndon B. Johnson National Historical Park")
* [Samuel Ealy Johnson Jr.](/wiki/Samuel_Ealy_Johnson_Jr. "Samuel Ealy Johnson Jr.") (father)
* [Southwest Texas State Teachers College](/wiki/Texas_State_University "Texas State University") ([BS](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Science "Bachelor of Science"))
* [United States Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy")   + [Naval Reserve](/wiki/United_States_Navy_Reserve "United States Navy Reserve")
* [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II")   + [Invasion of Salamaua–Lae](/wiki/Invasion_of_Salamaua%E2%80%93Lae "Invasion of Salamaua–Lae")

([/ˈlɪndən ˈbeɪnz/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); August 27, 1908 – January 22, 1973), also known as **LBJ**, was the 36th [president of the United States](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States"), serving from 1963 to 1969. He became president after the [assassination of John F. Kennedy](/wiki/Assassination_of_John_F._Kennedy "Assassination of John F. Kennedy"), under whom he had served as the 37th [vice president](/wiki/Vice_President_of_the_United_States "Vice President of the United States") from 1961 to 1963. A [Southern Democrat](/wiki/Southern_Democrat "Southern Democrat"), Johnson previously represented [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas") in [Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") for over 23 years, first as a [U.S. representative](/wiki/U.S._representative "U.S. representative") from 1937 to 1949, and then as a [U.S. senator](/wiki/U.S._senator "U.S. senator") from 1949 to 1961.

**Lyndon Baines Johnson** ([/ˈlɪndən ˈbeɪnz/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); August 27, 1908 – January 22, 1973), also known as **LBJ**, was the 36th [president of the United States](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States"), serving from 1963 to 1969. He became president after the [assassination of John F. Kennedy](/wiki/Assassination_of_John_F._Kennedy "Assassination of John F. Kennedy"), under whom he had served as the 37th [vice president](/wiki/Vice_President_of_the_United_States "Vice President of the United States") from 1961 to 1963. A [Southern Democrat](/wiki/Southern_Democrat "Southern Democrat"), Johnson previously represented [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas") in [Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") for over 23 years, first as a [U.S. representative](/wiki/U.S._representative "U.S. representative") from 1937 to 1949, and then as a [U.S. senator](/wiki/U.S._senator "U.S. senator") from 1949 to 1961.

Born in [Stonewall, Texas](/wiki/Stonewall,_Texas "Stonewall, Texas"), Johnson worked as a teacher and a congressional aide before winning election to the U.S. House of Representatives in 1937. In 1948, he was controversially declared the winner in the Democratic primary for the [U.S. Senate election](/wiki/1948_United_States_Senate_election_in_Texas "1948 United States Senate election in Texas") in Texas before winning the general election.[[1]](#cite_note-2) He became [Senate majority whip](/wiki/Party_leaders_of_the_United_States_Senate "Party leaders of the United States Senate") in 1951, Senate Democratic leader in 1953 and majority leader in 1954. Senator Kennedy bested Johnson and his other rivals for the [1960](/wiki/1960_Democratic_Party_presidential_primaries "1960 Democratic Party presidential primaries") Democratic presidential nomination before surprising many by offering to make Johnson his vice presidential running mate. The Kennedy–Johnson ticket won [the general election](/wiki/1960_United_States_presidential_election "1960 United States presidential election"). Vice President Johnson assumed the presidency in 1963, after President Kennedy was assassinated. The following year, Johnson [won reelection to the presidency in a landslide](/wiki/1964_United_States_presidential_election "1964 United States presidential election"), winning the largest share of the popular vote for the Democratic Party in history, and the highest for any candidate since the advent of widespread popular elections in the 1820s.

Johnson's domestic policy agenda known as the [Great Society](/wiki/Great_Society "Great Society") was aimed at expanding [civil rights](/wiki/Civil_rights_movement "Civil rights movement"), public broadcasting, access to health care, aid to education and the arts, urban and rural development, consumer protection, environmentalism, and public services. He sought to create better living conditions for low-income Americans by spearheading the [War on Poverty](/wiki/War_on_Poverty "War on Poverty"). As part of these efforts, Johnson signed the [Social Security Amendments of 1965](/wiki/Social_Security_Amendments_of_1965 "Social Security Amendments of 1965"), which resulted in the creation of [Medicare](/wiki/Medicare_(United_States) "Medicare (United States)") and [Medicaid](/wiki/Medicaid "Medicaid"). Johnson made the [Apollo Moon landing program](/wiki/Apollo_program "Apollo program") a national priority; enacted the [Higher Education Act of 1965](/wiki/Higher_Education_Act_of_1965 "Higher Education Act of 1965") which established federally insured student loans; and signed the [Immigration and Nationality Act of 1965](/wiki/Immigration_and_Nationality_Act_of_1965 "Immigration and Nationality Act of 1965") which laid the groundwork for U.S. immigration policy today. Johnson's civil rights legacy was shaped by the [Civil Rights Act of 1964](/wiki/Civil_Rights_Act_of_1964 "Civil Rights Act of 1964"), the [Voting Rights Act of 1965](/wiki/Voting_Rights_Act_of_1965 "Voting Rights Act of 1965"), and the [Civil Rights Act of 1968](/wiki/Civil_Rights_Act_of_1968 "Civil Rights Act of 1968"). Due to his domestic agenda, Johnson's presidency marked the peak of [modern American liberalism](/wiki/Modern_liberalism_in_the_United_States "Modern liberalism in the United States") in the 20th century.[[2]](#cite_note-3) Johnson's foreign policy prioritized [containment](/wiki/Containment "Containment") of [communism](/wiki/Communism "Communism"), including in the ongoing [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War").

Johnson began his presidency with near-universal support, but his approval declined throughout his presidency as the public became frustrated with both the Vietnam War and domestic unrest, including [race riots](/wiki/Ghetto_riots_(1964%E2%80%931969) "Ghetto riots (1964–1969)"), increasing public skepticism with his reports and policies (coined the *[credibility gap](/wiki/Credibility_gap "Credibility gap")*), and increasing crime. Johnson initially sought to run for re-election in 1968; however, following [disappointing results in the New Hampshire primary](/wiki/1968_Democratic_Party_presidential_primaries "1968 Democratic Party presidential primaries"), he [withdrew his candidacy](/wiki/Withdrawal_of_Lyndon_B._Johnson_from_the_1968_United_States_presidential_election "Withdrawal of Lyndon B. Johnson from the 1968 United States presidential election"). Johnson retired to his Texas ranch and kept a low public profile until he died in 1973. Public opinion and academic assessments of Johnson's legacy have fluctuated greatly. Historians and scholars [rank Johnson in the upper tier](/wiki/Historical_rankings_of_presidents_of_the_United_States "Historical rankings of presidents of the United States") for his accomplishments regarding domestic policy. His administration passed many major laws that made substantial changes in civil rights, health care, welfare, and education. Conversely, Johnson is heavily criticized for his foreign policy, namely escalating American involvement in the Vietnam War.[[3]](#cite_note-histeval-4)[[4]](#cite_note-5)

Lyndon Baines Johnson was born on August 27, 1908, near [Stonewall, Texas](/wiki/Stonewall,_Texas "Stonewall, Texas"), in a small farmhouse on the [Pedernales River](/wiki/Pedernales_River "Pedernales River").[[5]](#cite_note-6) He was the eldest of five children born to [Samuel Ealy Johnson Jr.](/wiki/Samuel_Ealy_Johnson_Jr. "Samuel Ealy Johnson Jr.") and Rebekah Baines.[[6]](#cite_note-7)[[7]](#cite_note-8) Johnson was not given a name until he was three months old, as his parents could not agree on a name that both liked. Finally, he was named after "criminal lawyer—a county lawyer" W. C. Linden, who his father liked; his mother agreed on the condition of spelling it as Lyndon.[[8]](#cite_note-LBJOnRecord-9)[[9]](#cite_note-10) Johnson had one brother, [Sam Houston Johnson](/wiki/Sam_Houston_Johnson "Sam Houston Johnson"), and three sisters, Rebekah, Josefa, and Lucia.[[10]](#cite_note-11) Through his mother, he was a great-grandson of [Baptist](/wiki/Baptists "Baptists") clergyman [George Washington Baines](/wiki/George_Washington_Baines "George Washington Baines").[[11]](#cite_note-12)

Johnson's paternal grandfather, [Samuel Ealy Johnson Sr.](/wiki/Samuel_Ealy_Johnson_Sr. "Samuel Ealy Johnson Sr."), was raised Baptist and for a time was a member of the [Christian Church (Disciples of Christ)](/wiki/Christian_Church_(Disciples_of_Christ) "Christian Church (Disciples of Christ)"). In his later years, Samuel Sr. became a [Christadelphian](/wiki/Christadelphian "Christadelphian"); Samuel Jr. also joined the Christadelphian Church toward the end of his life.[[12]](#cite_note-13) Johnson was influenced in his positive attitude toward Jews by the religious beliefs that [his family](/wiki/Family_of_Lyndon_B._Johnson "Family of Lyndon B. Johnson"), especially his grandfather, had shared with him.[[13]](#cite_note-14)

Johnson grew up poor, with his father losing a great deal of money.[[14]](#cite_note-povertyupbringing-15) Biographer [Robert Caro](/wiki/Robert_Caro "Robert Caro") described him as being raised "in a land without electricity, where the soil was so rocky that it was hard to earn a living from it."[[14]](#cite_note-povertyupbringing-15)

In school, Johnson was a talkative youth who was elected president of his 11th-grade class. He graduated in 1924 from [Johnson City High School](/wiki/Johnson_City_High_School "Johnson City High School"), where he participated in [public speaking](/wiki/Public_speaking "Public speaking"), [debate](/wiki/Debate "Debate"), and [baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball").[[15]](#cite_note-16)[[16]](#cite_note-17) At 15, Johnson was the youngest in his class. Pressured by his parents to attend college, he enrolled at a "sub college" of [Southwest Texas State Teachers College](/wiki/Southwest_Texas_State_Teachers_College "Southwest Texas State Teachers College") (SWTSTC) in the summer of 1924, where students from unaccredited high schools could take the 12th-grade courses needed for admission to college. He left the school just weeks after his arrival and decided to move to California. He worked at his cousin's legal practice and in odd jobs before returning to Texas, where he worked as a day laborer.[[17]](#cite_note-18)

In 1926, Johnson enrolled at SWTSTC. He worked his way through school, participated in debate and campus politics, and edited the school newspaper, [*The College Star*](/wiki/University_Star "University Star").[[18]](#cite_note-19) The college years refined his skills of persuasion and political organization. For nine months, from 1928 to 1929, Johnson paused his studies to teach [Mexican–American](/wiki/Mexican_Americans "Mexican Americans") children at the segregated Welhausen School in [Cotulla, Texas](/wiki/Cotulla,_Texas "Cotulla, Texas"), 90 miles (140 km) south of [San Antonio](/wiki/San_Antonio "San Antonio"). The job helped him to save money to complete his education, and he graduated in 1930 with a Bachelor of Science in history and his certificate of qualification as a high school teacher.[[19]](#cite_note-20)[[20]](#cite_note-21) He briefly taught at Pearsall High School in [Pearsall, Texas](/wiki/Pearsall,_Texas "Pearsall, Texas") before taking a position teaching public speaking at [Sam Houston High School](/wiki/Sam_Houston_High_School_(Houston,_Texas) "Sam Houston High School (Houston, Texas)") in Houston.[[21]](#cite_note-22)

When he returned to San Marcos in 1965, after signing the [Higher Education Act of 1965](/wiki/Higher_Education_Act_of_1965 "Higher Education Act of 1965"), Johnson reminisced:

> I shall never forget the faces of the boys and the girls in that little Welhausen Mexican School, and I remember even yet the pain of realizing and knowing then that college was closed to practically every one of those children because they were too poor. And I think it was then that I made up my mind that this nation could never rest while the door to knowledge remained closed to any American.[[22]](#cite_note-23)

## Entry into politics

After [Richard M. Kleberg](/wiki/Richard_M._Kleberg "Richard M. Kleberg") won a 1931 special election to represent Texas in the [United States House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives"), he appointed Johnson as his legislative secretary. This marked Johnson's formal introduction to politics. Johnson secured the position on the recommendation of his father and that of state senator Welly Hopkins, for whom Johnson had campaigned in 1930.[[23]](#cite_note-24) Kleberg had little interest in the day-to-day duties of a Congressman, instead delegating them to Johnson.[[24]](#cite_note-25) After [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt") won the [1932 U.S. presidential election](/wiki/1932_U.S._presidential_election "1932 U.S. presidential election"), Johnson became a lifelong supporter of Roosevelt's [New Deal](/wiki/New_Deal "New Deal").[[25]](#cite_note-26) Johnson was elected speaker of the "Little Congress", a group of Congressional aides, where he cultivated Congressmen, newspapermen, and lobbyists. Johnson's friends soon included aides to President Roosevelt as well as fellow Texans such as vice president [John Nance Garner](/wiki/John_Nance_Garner "John Nance Garner") and congressman [Sam Rayburn](/wiki/Sam_Rayburn "Sam Rayburn").[[26]](#cite_note-27)

In 1935, Johnson was hired as Texas state administrator of the [National Youth Administration](/wiki/National_Youth_Administration "National Youth Administration") (NYA). The job allowed him to travel around Texas seeking financial sponsors for NYA [construction projects](/wiki/Construction_project "Construction project"). Within six months, 18,000 young Texans were working on roads, parks, schools, and other public buildings.[[27]](#cite_note-Biography:_Lyndon_B._Johnson-28) He resigned two years later to run for Congress. A notoriously tough boss, Johnson often demanded long workdays and work on weekends.[[28]](#cite_note-29) He was described by friends, fellow politicians, and historians as motivated by lust for power and control. As Caro observes, "Johnson's ambition was uncommon – in the degree to which it was unencumbered by even the slightest excess weight of ideology, of philosophy, of principles, of beliefs."[[29]](#cite_note-30)

## U.S. House of Representatives (1937–1949)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Franklin_D._Roosevelt%2C_Lyndon_B._Johnson_%26_James_Allred%2C_May_1937.png/250px-Franklin_D._Roosevelt%2C_Lyndon_B._Johnson_%26_James_Allred%2C_May_1937.png)](/wiki/File:Franklin_D._Roosevelt,_Lyndon_B._Johnson_%26_James_Allred,_May_1937.png)

President [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt") (left), Texas governor [James Burr V Allred](/wiki/James_Burr_V_Allred "James Burr V Allred") (center), and Johnson (right) in 1937; Johnson later used an edited version of this photo with Allred airbrushed out in his [1941 senatorial campaign](/wiki/1941_United_States_Senate_special_election_in_Texas "1941 United States Senate special election in Texas").[[30]](#cite_note-31)

In 1937, after the death of 13-term congressman [James P. Buchanan](/wiki/James_P._Buchanan "James P. Buchanan"), Johnson successfully campaigned in a special election for [Texas's 10th congressional district](/wiki/Texas%27s_10th_congressional_district "Texas's 10th congressional district"), which included [Austin](/wiki/Austin,_Texas "Austin, Texas") and the surrounding [Texas Hill Country](/wiki/Texas_Hill_Country "Texas Hill Country"). He ran on a [New Deal](/wiki/New_Deal "New Deal") platform and was effectively aided by his wife.[[31]](#cite_note-32) He served as a U.S. Representative from April 10, 1937, to January 3, 1949.[[32]](#cite_note-Bioguide.congress.gov-33) President Roosevelt found Johnson to be a political ally[[33]](#cite_note-34) and conduit for information, particularly regarding the internal politics of Texas and the machinations of Vice President [John Nance Garner](/wiki/John_Nance_Garner "John Nance Garner") and [House Speaker](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_U.S._House_of_Representatives "Speaker of the U.S. House of Representatives") [Sam Rayburn](/wiki/Sam_Rayburn "Sam Rayburn").[[34]](#cite_note-35) As a member of the [House Naval Affairs Committee](/wiki/United_States_House_Committee_on_Naval_Affairs "United States House Committee on Naval Affairs"), Johnson helped plan a huge [naval air training base](/wiki/Naval_Air_Station_Corpus_Christi "Naval Air Station Corpus Christi") at [Corpus Christi, Texas](/wiki/Corpus_Christi,_Texas "Corpus Christi, Texas"). He also helped establish [shipbuilding](/wiki/Shipbuilding "Shipbuilding") sites at [Houston](/wiki/Houston "Houston") and [Orange, Texas](/wiki/Orange,_Texas "Orange, Texas"); and a [Navy Reserve](/wiki/United_States_Navy_Reserve "United States Navy Reserve") station in [Dallas](/wiki/Dallas "Dallas").[[35]](#cite_note-36)

Johnson successfully sponsored many federal projects for his home district. One was a program that provided cheap electricity for farmers under the new [Rural Electrification Administration](/wiki/Rural_Electrification_Administration "Rural Electrification Administration") (REA), and he secured approval to complete the hydroelectric [Mansfield Dam](/wiki/Mansfield_Dam "Mansfield Dam") on the [Colorado River](/wiki/Colorado_River_(Texas) "Colorado River (Texas)") near Austin.[[27]](#cite_note-Biography:_Lyndon_B._Johnson-28)[[36]](#cite_note-37) He also sponsored projects that gave his Texas district [soil conservation](/wiki/Soil_conservation "Soil conservation"), [public housing](/wiki/Public_housing "Public housing"), lower [railroad freight rates](/wiki/Freight_rate "Freight rate"), and expanded credit for loans to farmers.[[37]](#cite_note-Dallek,_Robert_pp._170-38) He steered the projects towards contractors he knew, such as [Herman and George Brown](/wiki/Kellogg,_Brown_and_Root#Brown_&_Root "Kellogg, Brown and Root"), who financed much of Johnson's future career.[[38]](#cite_note-39)[[39]](#cite_note-40)

During this time Johnson maintained a hostile position towards [civil rights](/wiki/Civil_rights "Civil rights") legislation like almost all other Southern Democrat legislators; voting against anti-[lynching](/wiki/Lynching_in_the_United_States "Lynching in the United States") legislation, anti-[poll tax](/wiki/Poll_taxes_in_the_United_States "Poll taxes in the United States") legislation and the [Fair Employment Practice Committee](/wiki/Fair_Employment_Practice_Committee "Fair Employment Practice Committee").[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaro2002Ch._31._The_Compassion_of_Lyndon_Johnson-41)[[41]](#cite_note-42)

### 1941 U.S. Senate election

Main article: [1941 United States Senate special election in Texas](/wiki/1941_United_States_Senate_special_election_in_Texas "1941 United States Senate special election in Texas")

In April 1941, [U.S. Senator](/wiki/U.S._Senator "U.S. Senator") [Morris Sheppard](/wiki/Morris_Sheppard "Morris Sheppard") of Texas died, triggering a [special election](/wiki/1941_United_States_Senate_special_election_in_Texas "1941 United States Senate special election in Texas"). Texas law allowed Johnson to run without giving up his House seat. The election had no primaries or runoff, forcing Johnson to compete against multiple Democrats, including Governor [W. Lee "Pappy" O'Daniel](/wiki/W._Lee_O%27Daniel "W. Lee O'Daniel").[[42]](#cite_note-Sweany-2006-43)

Johnson campaigned aggressively, emphasizing his ties to President Roosevelt, and initially appeared to be leading. However, after late returns came in, O'Daniel narrowly won by 1,311 votes.[[42]](#cite_note-Sweany-2006-43) In [*The Path to Power*](/wiki/The_Years_of_Lyndon_Johnson#The_Path_to_Power "The Years of Lyndon Johnson"), Johnson biographer [Robert Caro](/wiki/Robert_Caro "Robert Caro") argues that the election results were manipulated in O'Daniel's favor by lobbyists from Texas' [alcohol industry](/wiki/Alcohol_industry "Alcohol industry"). O'Daniel was a staunch [prohibitionist](/wiki/Prohibition_in_the_United_States "Prohibition in the United States"), and as Governor, he had proposed a bill preventing the sale of alcohol within ten miles of a military base; Caro notes how business interests feared the passing of this bill, as preparations for [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") had brought thousands of young soldiers into the region. To prevent the passing of this bill, lobbyists sought to elect O'Daniel to the Senate to place the "wet" lieutenant governor [Coke Stevenson](/wiki/Coke_Stevenson "Coke Stevenson") in the governorship, and hence rigged ballots in East Texas, swinging the election in O'Daniel's favor.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaro1982734–740-44) Johnson ultimately lost by just 0.23% of the vote.[[44]](#cite_note-45)

Though defeated, Johnson retained his House seat and political network, including support from South Texas political boss [George Berham Parr](/wiki/George_Berham_Parr "George Berham Parr").[[45]](#cite_note-Dallek_1991-46) When O'Daniel declined to seek re-election in 1948,[[46]](#cite_note-47) Johnson prepared for another Senate run, this time carefully managing vote tallies.[[45]](#cite_note-Dallek_1991-46) He would go on to win the 1948 Democratic primary by just 87 votes.[[47]](#cite_note-48)

### Active military duty (1941–1942)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Portrait_of_Lyndon_B._Johnson_in_Navy_Uniform_-_42-3-7_-_03-1942.jpg/250px-Portrait_of_Lyndon_B._Johnson_in_Navy_Uniform_-_42-3-7_-_03-1942.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portrait_of_Lyndon_B._Johnson_in_Navy_Uniform_-_42-3-7_-_03-1942.jpg)

Johnson as a lieutenant commander in the [United States Navy Reserve](/wiki/United_States_Navy_Reserve "United States Navy Reserve") in March 1942

Johnson was a member of the [U.S. Naval Reserve](/wiki/U.S._Naval_Reserve "U.S. Naval Reserve") when [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") [attacked Pearl Harbor](/wiki/Attacked_Pearl_Harbor "Attacked Pearl Harbor") in December 1941. Two days after the attack, he requested an indefinite leave of absence and applied for active duty, making him one of the first members of Congress to volunteer for an active military role.[[48]](#cite_note-LBJ's_Military_Service-49)

Johnson was first ordered to the Office of the Chief of Naval Operations to assess the production and manpower problems that were slowing the manufacture of ships and planes. In May 1942, he was appointed President Roosevelt's personal representative to the Navy and was sent on a survey mission of the [Pacific theater](/wiki/Pacific_War "Pacific War") of combat. Stationed in [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") and [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"), it was his duty to find the underreported problems facing U.S. troops in the Pacific and report this firsthand information to Roosevelt.[[48]](#cite_note-LBJ's_Military_Service-49)

On June 9, 1942, Johnson received the [Silver Star](/wiki/Silver_Star "Silver Star") from General [Douglas MacArthur](/wiki/Douglas_MacArthur "Douglas MacArthur") for gallantry in action during an aerial combat mission over hostile positions in [New Guinea](/wiki/New_Guinea "New Guinea").[[49]](#cite_note-50) Though accounts differ, Johnson's aircraft, a B-26 bomber, reportedly turned back before reaching the target due to mechanical trouble.[[50]](#cite_note-CNN_Special-51)[[51]](#cite_note-theguardian.com-52) Johnson's biographer Robert Caro was quoted as saying "I think that the weight of the evidence at this moment is that the plane was attacked by [Zeroes](/wiki/Mitsubishi_A6M_Zero "Mitsubishi A6M Zero") and that he was cool under fire",[[50]](#cite_note-CNN_Special-51) but also "The fact is, LBJ never got within sight of Japanese forces. His combat experience was a myth."[[52]](#cite_note-USNI-53)

Johnson served in the Pacific through July 1942. He was released from his military active duty after President Roosevelt recalled all active duty legislators back to Washington, D.C.[[48]](#cite_note-LBJ's_Military_Service-49) In addition to the Silver Star, Johnson received the [Asiatic-Pacific Campaign Medal](/wiki/Asiatic-Pacific_Campaign_Medal "Asiatic-Pacific Campaign Medal"), and the [World War II Victory Medal](/wiki/World_War_II_Victory_Medal "World War II Victory Medal").[[48]](#cite_note-LBJ's_Military_Service-49)

## U.S. Senate (1949–1961)

### 1948 U.S. Senate election

Main article: [1948 United States Senate election in Texas](/wiki/1948_United_States_Senate_election_in_Texas "1948 United States Senate election in Texas")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Texas_U.S._Senate_Democratic_primary_runoff%2C_1948.svg/250px-Texas_U.S._Senate_Democratic_primary_runoff%2C_1948.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Texas_U.S._Senate_Democratic_primary_runoff,_1948.svg)

Results of the Democratic runoff primary in the [1948 U.S. Senate election in Texas](/wiki/1948_United_States_Senate_election_in_Texas "1948 United States Senate election in Texas") by county. Johnson squeaked out a controversial victory by 87 votes.[[53]](#cite_note-auto1-54) Johnson counties appear in blue, and Stevenson counties in green.[[54]](#cite_note-55)

In [1948](/wiki/1948_United_States_Senate_election_in_Texas "1948 United States Senate election in Texas"), Johnson again ran for the U.S. Senate and won the general election after being declared winner in a highly controversial Democratic Party [primary election](/wiki/Partisan_primary "Partisan primary") against the well-known former governor [Coke Stevenson](/wiki/Coke_Stevenson "Coke Stevenson").[[55]](#cite_note-auto-56) Johnson drew crowds to fairgrounds with his rented [Sikorsky S-51](/wiki/Sikorsky_S-51 "Sikorsky S-51") helicopter, dubbed "The Johnson City Windmill".[[56]](#cite_note-57) He raised money to flood the state with campaign circulars and won over conservatives by casting doubts on Stevenson's support for the [Taft–Hartley Act](/wiki/Taft%E2%80%93Hartley_Act "Taft–Hartley Act") (curbing union power).[[57]](#cite_note-58) Stevenson came in first in the primary but lacked a majority, so a runoff election was held;[[55]](#cite_note-auto-56)[[58]](#cite_note-59) Johnson campaigned harder, while Stevenson's efforts slumped due to a lack of funds.[[59]](#cite_note-60)

The runoff vote count, handled by the Democratic State Central Committee, took a week. Johnson was announced the winner by 87 votes out of 988,295, an extremely narrow margin. However, Johnson's victory was based on 200 "patently fraudulent"[[60]](#cite_note-baum-61): 608 ballots reported six days after the election from [Box 13](/wiki/Box_13_scandal "Box 13 scandal") in [Jim Wells County](/wiki/Jim_Wells_County,_Texas "Jim Wells County, Texas"), in an area dominated by political boss [George Berham Parr](/wiki/George_Berham_Parr "George Berham Parr"). The added names were in alphabetical order and written with the same pen and handwriting, at the end of the list of voters. Some on this part of the list insisted that they had not voted that day.[[61]](#cite_note-62) Election judge Luis Salas said in 1977 that he had certified 202 fraudulent ballots, 200 for Johnson, and two for Stevenson.[[62]](#cite_note-'70s-63) [Robert Caro](/wiki/Robert_Caro "Robert Caro") made the case in his 1990 book that Johnson had stolen the election in Jim Wells County, and that there were thousands of fraudulent votes in other counties as well, including 10,000 votes switched in [San Antonio](/wiki/San_Antonio "San Antonio").[[63]](#cite_note-64) The Democratic State Central Committee voted to certify Johnson's nomination by a majority of one (29–28). The state Democratic convention upheld Johnson. Stevenson went to court, eventually taking his case before the [U.S. Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States"), but with timely help from his friend and future U.S. Supreme Court Justice [Abe Fortas](/wiki/Abe_Fortas "Abe Fortas"), Johnson prevailed on the basis that jurisdiction over naming a nominee rested with the party, not the federal government. Johnson soundly defeated [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") [Jack Porter](/wiki/Homa_J._Porter "Homa J. Porter") in the general election in November and went to Washington, permanently dubbed "Landslide Lyndon". Johnson, dismissive of his critics, happily adopted the nickname.[[64]](#cite_note-65)

### Freshman senator to majority whip

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Senator_Lyndon_Johnson.jpg/250px-Senator_Lyndon_Johnson.jpg)](/wiki/File:Senator_Lyndon_Johnson.jpg)

Johnson's [United States Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate") portrait in the 1950s

During his two terms in the Senate, Johnson drifted rightward.[[65]](#cite_note-66) He felt he had to tread carefully lest he offend politically powerful conservative [oil and gas interests in Texas](/wiki/Texas_oil_boom "Texas oil boom"),[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson1996530-67) and in part to curry favor with the chamber's powerful southern chairmen, most notably Senator [Richard Russell](/wiki/Richard_Russell_Jr. "Richard Russell Jr."), Democrat from Georgia and leader of the [Southern Caucus](/wiki/Southern_Caucus "Southern Caucus")[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaro2002Ch._7._A_Russell_of_the_Russells_of_Georgia-68) within the [conservative coalition](/wiki/Conservative_coalition "Conservative coalition") that dominated the Senate.[[68]](#cite_note-69) With Russell's support, Johnson won election as Democratic whip in 1951, serving in this capacity until 1953.[[32]](#cite_note-Bioguide.congress.gov-33) While serving as whip, Johnson increased his ability to persuade people to reach agreement.[[69]](#cite_note-70)

As a member of the [Senate Committee on Interstate and Foreign Commerce](/wiki/Senate_Committee_on_Interstate_and_Foreign_Commerce "Senate Committee on Interstate and Foreign Commerce"), Johnson was chairman of the Senate subcommittee that [refused](/wiki/Leland_Olds_hearing "Leland Olds hearing") the re-nomination of [Leland Olds](/wiki/Leland_Olds "Leland Olds")—who was opposed by Texas oil and gas interests[[70]](#cite_note-71)—as chairman of the [Federal Power Commission](/wiki/Federal_Power_Commission "Federal Power Commission") (FPC) on the grounds that he had been sympathetic towards communism.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaro2002Ch._10–12-72) As a member of the FPC, Olds had played a key role in the development of federal regulation of the [natural gas industry](/wiki/Natural_gas_industry "Natural gas industry").[[72]](#cite_note-73)

President Truman favored federal control of [tidelands oil deposits](/wiki/Offshore_oil_and_gas_in_the_United_States "Offshore oil and gas in the United States") off the Texas coast. Privately, Johnson told friends that he favored federal control, but in public he staunchly defended [states' rights](/wiki/States%27_rights "States' rights"). In 1951–52, Johnson helped pass bills granting 47 states control over 3 miles of offshore lands and Texas, 10.5 miles. Truman vetoed the bills, calling them "robbery in broad daylight".[[73]](#cite_note-74)

Johnson was appointed to the [Senate Armed Services Committee](/wiki/United_States_Senate_Committee_on_Armed_Services "United States Senate Committee on Armed Services"),[[74]](#cite_note-75) and became increasingly concerned with the country's military preparedness in the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") with the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"). He became chairman of the [Senate Preparedness Investigating Subcommittee](/wiki/Senate_Preparedness_Investigating_Subcommittee "Senate Preparedness Investigating Subcommittee"), and conducted investigations of defense costs and efficiency.[[75]](#cite_note-76) After the [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War") began in 1950, he called for more troops and for improved weapons.[[76]](#cite_note-77) Biographer Caro wrote that Johnson was obsessed with having all subcommittee reports be unanimous, maneuvering frequently to gain the backing of all the Republicans.[[77]](#cite_note-78) He used his political influence in the Senate to receive broadcast licenses from the [Federal Communications Commission](/wiki/Federal_Communications_Commission "Federal Communications Commission") in his wife's name.[[62]](#cite_note-'70s-63)[[78]](#cite_note-new_yorker-79)

### Senate Democratic leader

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Lyndon_Johnson_and_Richard_Russell_cropped.jpg/250px-Lyndon_Johnson_and_Richard_Russell_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lyndon_Johnson_and_Richard_Russell_cropped.jpg)

President Johnson giving "The Treatment" to U.S. Senator [Richard Russell Jr.](/wiki/Richard_Russell_Jr. "Richard Russell Jr.") in 1963

In the [1952 elections](/wiki/1952_United_States_Senate_elections "1952 United States Senate elections"), Republicans won a majority in both the House and Senate. In January 1953, Johnson was chosen by his fellow Democrats as Senate [Minority Leader](/wiki/Minority_Leader "Minority Leader"); he became the most junior senator ever elected to this position.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson1996529-80) He reformed the seniority system so that Democratic senators, including freshmen, were more likely to receive a committee assignment that closely aligned with their expertise rather than an assignment based solely on their seniority.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaro2002-81)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

#### Senate Majority Leader

In [1954](/wiki/1954_United_States_Senate_election "1954 United States Senate election"), Johnson was re-elected to the Senate and, with Democrats winning the majority in the Senate, he became [majority leader](/wiki/Majority_leader "Majority leader").[[55]](#cite_note-auto-56) President [Dwight D. Eisenhower](/wiki/Dwight_D._Eisenhower "Dwight D. Eisenhower") found Johnson more cooperative than the Senate Republican leader, [William F. Knowland](/wiki/William_F._Knowland "William F. Knowland") of California. Particularly on foreign policy, Johnson offered bipartisan support to the president.[[81]](#cite_note-82)[[82]](#cite_note-83)

Historians Caro and Dallek consider Johnson the most effective Senate majority leader ever.[[83]](#cite_note-84)[[84]](#cite_note-CaroSite-85) He was unusually proficient at gathering information.[[85]](#cite_note-86) One biographer suggests he was "the greatest intelligence gatherer Washington has ever known", discovering exactly where every senator stood on issues, his philosophy and prejudices, his strengths and weaknesses, and what it took to get his vote.[[86]](#cite_note-87) [Bobby Baker](/wiki/Bobby_Baker "Bobby Baker") claimed that Johnson would occasionally send senators on [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") trips so they were absent and unable to cast dissenting votes.[[87]](#cite_note-88) Central to Johnson's control was "The Treatment",[[88]](#cite_note-89) described by two journalists:

> The Treatment could last ten minutes or four hours. It came, enveloping its target, at the Johnson Ranch swimming pool, in one of Johnson's offices, in the Senate cloakroom, on the floor of the Senate itself – wherever Johnson might find a fellow Senator within his reach.
> Its tone could be supplication, accusation, cajolery, exuberance, scorn, tears, complaint, and the hint of threat. It was all of these together. It ran the gamut of human emotions. Its velocity was breathtaking and it was all in one direction. Interjections from the target were rare. Johnson anticipated them before they could be spoken. He moved in close, his face a scant millimeter from his target, his eyes widening and narrowing, his eyebrows rising and falling. From his pockets poured clippings, memos, statistics. Mimicry, humor, and the genius of analogy made The Treatment an almost hypnotic experience and rendered the target stunned and helpless.[[89]](#cite_note-90)

In 1956, during the [Suez Crisis](/wiki/Suez_Crisis "Suez Crisis"), Johnson tried to prevent the U.S. government from criticizing [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") for its invasion of the [Sinai Peninsula](/wiki/Sinai_Peninsula "Sinai Peninsula").[[90]](#cite_note-91) Along with much of the rest of the nation, Johnson was appalled by the threat of possible Soviet domination of [space exploration](/wiki/Space_exploration "Space exploration") implied by the launch of *[Sputnik 1](/wiki/Sputnik_1 "Sputnik 1")*, the first artificial Earth [satellite](/wiki/Satellite "Satellite"), and used his influence to ensure passage of the [National Aeronautics and Space Act of 1958](/wiki/National_Aeronautics_and_Space_Act "National Aeronautics and Space Act"), which established [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA").[[53]](#cite_note-auto1-54) Johnson helped establish the [Senate Aeronautical and Space Committee](/wiki/United_States_Senate_Committee_on_Aeronautical_and_Space_Sciences "United States Senate Committee on Aeronautical and Space Sciences"), and made himself its first chairman.[[91]](#cite_note-92)

During his tenure as Majority Leader, Johnson did not sign the 1956 [Southern Manifesto](/wiki/Southern_Manifesto "Southern Manifesto"),[[92]](#cite_note-93)[[93]](#cite_note-94) and shepherded the [Civil Rights Acts of 1957](/wiki/Civil_Rights_Act_of_1957 "Civil Rights Act of 1957") and [1960](/wiki/Civil_Rights_Act_of_1960 "Civil Rights Act of 1960") to passage—the first civil rights bills to pass Congress since the [Enforcement Acts](/wiki/Enforcement_Acts "Enforcement Acts") and the [Civil Rights Act of 1875](/wiki/Civil_Rights_Act_of_1875 "Civil Rights Act of 1875") during [Reconstruction](/wiki/Reconstruction_era "Reconstruction era").[[99]](#cite_note-1957_&_1960_civil_rights_bills-100) Johnson negotiated a middle course between Northern liberal senators and the Southern bloc of senators who had opposed such legislation by removing key enforcement provisions,[[100]](#cite_note-101)[[101]](#cite_note-102) such as Title III, which authorized the [attorney general](/wiki/Attorney_general "Attorney general") to initiate civil action for preventive relief in a wide range of civil rights matters.[[102]](#cite_note-103) Being a Southerner was seen as an impossible barrier for a presidential candidate[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECaro2002Ch._5._The_Path_Ahead-104) and towards the end of his Senate career as well as not signing the Southern Manifesto, he distanced himself further from the Southern Caucus in 1959 by joining the Democrat's Western regional conference.[[104]](#cite_note-TimeWest-105)

## Campaigns of 1960

See also: [1960 United States presidential election](/wiki/1960_United_States_presidential_election "1960 United States presidential election")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Lbj1960_copy.gif/250px-Lbj1960_copy.gif)](/wiki/File:Lbj1960_copy.gif)

1960 presidential campaign logo

In [1960](/wiki/1960_United_States_presidential_election "1960 United States presidential election"), Johnson's success in the Senate rendered him a potential Democratic presidential candidate. [James H. Rowe](/wiki/James_H._Rowe "James H. Rowe") repeatedly urged Johnson to launch a campaign in early 1959, but Johnson thought it was better to wait, thinking that Senator [John F. Kennedy](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy "John F. Kennedy")'s candidacy would create a division in the ranks that could then be exploited.[[105]](#cite_note-CaroPassage1-106) Johnson's strategy was to sit out the [primaries](/wiki/1960_Democratic_Party_presidential_primaries "1960 Democratic Party presidential primaries") and to rely on his legislative record as [Senate Majority Leader](/wiki/Senate_Majority_Leader "Senate Majority Leader"), the support of [Southern Democrats](/wiki/Southern_Democrats "Southern Democrats"), and the favors owed by Democratic senators to him and by Democratic representatives to his close ally Sam Rayburn, the Speaker of the House.[[105]](#cite_note-CaroPassage1-106)
In July 1960, Johnson finally entered the campaign. Johnson's late entry, coupled with his reluctance to leave Washington, D.C., allowed rival John F. Kennedy to secure a substantial early lead in securing support from Democratic state party officials. Johnson underestimated Kennedy's endearing charm and intelligence in comparison to his perceived crude and wheeling-dealing "Landslide Lyndon" style.[[106]](#cite_note-107) Caro suggests that Johnson's hesitancy to enter the race resulted from his fear of losing.[[107]](#cite_note-108)

Johnson attempted in vain to capitalize on Kennedy's youth, poor health, and failure to take a position regarding [McCarthyism](/wiki/McCarthyism "McCarthyism").[[108]](#cite_note-109) He had formed a "Stop Kennedy" coalition with [Adlai Stevenson](/wiki/Adlai_Stevenson_II "Adlai Stevenson II"), [Stuart Symington](/wiki/Stuart_Symington "Stuart Symington"), and [Hubert Humphrey](/wiki/Hubert_Humphrey "Hubert Humphrey"), but it proved a failure. Despite Johnson having the support of established Democrats and the party leadership, this did not translate into popular approval. Johnson received 409 votes on the only ballot at the Democratic convention to Kennedy's 806, and so the convention nominated Kennedy. [Tip O'Neill](/wiki/Tip_O%27Neill "Tip O'Neill") was a representative from Kennedy's home state of [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts") at that time, and he recalled that Johnson approached him at the convention and said, "Tip, I know you have to support Kennedy at the start, but I'd like to have you with me on the second ballot." O'Neill replied, "Senator, there's not going to be any second ballot."[[109]](#cite_note-110)

### Vice presidential nomination

Main article: [1960 Democratic Party vice presidential candidate selection](/wiki/1960_Democratic_Party_vice_presidential_candidate_selection "1960 Democratic Party vice presidential candidate selection")

After much discussion with party leaders and others, Kennedy offered Johnson the vice presidential nomination at the [Los Angeles Biltmore Hotel](/wiki/Millennium_Biltmore_Hotel "Millennium Biltmore Hotel") on July 14, the morning after Kennedy was nominated, and Johnson accepted. From that point to the actual nomination that evening, several facts are in dispute, including whether convention chairman [LeRoy Collins](/wiki/LeRoy_Collins "LeRoy Collins")' had the two-thirds majority required to begin the convention's proceedings.[[110]](#cite_note-111) Kennedy's choice of Johnson as his running mate was intended to attract Southern votes.[[111]](#cite_note-112) Kennedy was a liberal [Bostonian](/wiki/Boston "Boston") and a [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic"). Johnson was more conservative, a [Southerner](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States"), and a member of the [Disciples of Christ](/wiki/Disciples_of_Christ "Disciples of Christ").[[112]](#cite_note-lawrence-113)[[113]](#cite_note-114) Nevertheless, labor leaders were unanimous in their opposition to Johnson. [AFL-CIO](/wiki/AFL-CIO "AFL-CIO") president [George Meany](/wiki/George_Meany "George Meany") called Johnson "the arch-foe of labor", and Illinois AFL-CIO president [Reuben Soderstrom](/wiki/Reuben_Soderstrom "Reuben Soderstrom") asserted Kennedy had "made chumps out of leaders of the American labor movement".[[114]](#cite_note-115)[[115]](#cite_note-116)

### Re-election to U.S. Senate

At the same time as his vice presidential run, Johnson also sought a third term in the U.S. Senate. According to Robert Caro:

> Johnson won an election for both the vice presidency of the United States, on the Kennedy–Johnson ticket, and for a third term as senator (he had Texas law changed to allow him to run for both offices). When he won the vice presidency, he made arrangements to resign from the Senate, as he was required to do under federal law, as soon as it convened on January 3, 1961.[[116]](#cite_note-117)

Johnson was [re-elected senator](/wiki/1960_United_States_Senate_election_in_Texas "1960 United States Senate election in Texas") with 1,306,605 votes (58 percent) to Republican [John Tower](/wiki/John_Tower "John Tower")'s 927,653 (41.1 percent). Fellow Democrat [William A. Blakley](/wiki/William_A._Blakley "William A. Blakley") was appointed to replace Johnson, but lost a [special election](/wiki/1961_United_States_Senate_special_election_in_Texas "1961 United States Senate special election in Texas") in May 1961 to Tower.

## Vice presidency (1961–1963)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/President_Kennedy_and_Vice_President_Johnson_prior_to_ceremony.jpg/250px-President_Kennedy_and_Vice_President_Johnson_prior_to_ceremony.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Kennedy_and_Vice_President_Johnson_prior_to_ceremony.jpg)

President [John F. Kennedy](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy "John F. Kennedy") and Vice President Johnson outside the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House") in August 1961

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Photograph_of_White_House_Meeting_with_Civil_Rights_Leaders._June_22%2C_1963_-_NARA_-_194190_%28no_border%29.tif/lossy-page1-250px-Photograph_of_White_House_Meeting_with_Civil_Rights_Leaders._June_22%2C_1963_-_NARA_-_194190_%28no_border%29.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:Photograph_of_White_House_Meeting_with_Civil_Rights_Leaders._June_22,_1963_-_NARA_-_194190_(no_border).tif)

Vice President Johnson and Attorney General [Robert Kennedy](/wiki/Robert_Kennedy "Robert Kennedy") meeting with civil rights leaders at the White House on June 22, 1963

See also: [Presidency of John F. Kennedy](/wiki/Presidency_of_John_F._Kennedy "Presidency of John F. Kennedy")

After the election, Johnson was concerned about the traditionally ineffective nature of his new office and sought authority not allotted to him as vice president. He initially sought a transfer of the authority of Senate majority leader to the vice presidency, since that office made him president of the Senate, but faced vehement opposition from the Democratic Caucus, including members whom he had counted as his supporters.[[117]](#cite_note-118)

Johnson sought to increase his influence within the executive branch. He drafted an executive order for Kennedy's signature, granting Johnson "general supervision" over matters of national security, and requiring all government agencies to "cooperate fully with the vice president in the carrying out of these assignments". Kennedy's response was to sign a non-binding letter requesting Johnson to "review" national security policies instead.[[118]](#cite_note-119) Kennedy similarly turned down early requests from Johnson to be given an office adjacent to the Oval Office and to employ a full-time staff within the White House.[[119]](#cite_note-120) In 1961, Kennedy appointed Johnson's friend [Sarah T. Hughes](/wiki/Sarah_T._Hughes "Sarah T. Hughes") to a federal judgeship. Johnson tried but failed to have Hughes nominated at the beginning of his vice presidency. House Speaker Sam Rayburn wangled the appointment from Kennedy in exchange for support of an administration bill.

With the exception of Kennedy himself, other members of the Kennedy White House were openly contemptuous of Johnson, including the president's brother, [Attorney General](/wiki/Attorney_General_of_the_United_States "Attorney General of the United States") [Robert F. Kennedy](/wiki/Robert_F._Kennedy "Robert F. Kennedy"), and they ridiculed his comparatively brusque and crude manner. Then Congressman [Tip O'Neill](/wiki/Tip_O%27Neill "Tip O'Neill") recalled that the Kennedy staffers "had a disdain for Johnson that they didn't even try to hide.... They actually took pride in snubbing him."[[120]](#cite_note-121)

Kennedy made efforts to keep Johnson busy and informed, telling aides, "I can't afford to have my vice president, who knows every reporter in Washington, going around saying we're all screwed up, so we're going to keep him happy."[[121]](#cite_note-122) Kennedy appointed him to jobs such as the head of the President's Committee on [Equal Employment Opportunities](/wiki/Equal_Employment_Opportunity_Commission "Equal Employment Opportunity Commission"), where Johnson worked with African Americans and other minorities. Kennedy may have intended this to remain a nominal position, but [Taylor Branch](/wiki/Taylor_Branch "Taylor Branch") contends in his 1998 book *[Pillar of Fire](/wiki/America_in_the_King_Years "America in the King Years")* that Johnson pushed the Kennedy administration's actions further and faster for civil rights than Kennedy originally intended.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Johnson went on multiple minor diplomatic missions, which gave him some insights into global issues and opportunities for self-promotion. During his visit to [West Berlin](/wiki/West_Berlin "West Berlin") on August 19–20, 1961, Johnson sought to calm Berliners who were outraged by the building of the [Berlin Wall](/wiki/Berlin_Wall "Berlin Wall").[[122]](#cite_note-123) He also attended Cabinet and [National Security Council](/wiki/United_States_National_Security_Council "United States National Security Council") meetings. Kennedy gave Johnson control over all presidential appointments involving Texas, and appointed him chairman of the President's Ad Hoc Committee for Science.[[123]](#cite_note-124)

Kennedy also appointed Johnson Chairman of the [National Aeronautics and Space Council](/wiki/National_Space_Council "National Space Council"). The Soviets beat the United States with [the first crewed spaceflight](/wiki/Vostok_1 "Vostok 1") in April 1961, and Kennedy gave Johnson the task of evaluating the U.S. space program and recommending a project that would allow the United States to catch up or beat the Soviets.[[124]](#cite_note-125) Johnson recommended that the United States gain the leadership role by committing to [landing an American on the Moon in the 1960s](/wiki/Apollo_program "Apollo program").[[125]](#cite_note-lbjmemo-126)[[126]](#cite_note-127) Kennedy assigned priority to the space program, but Johnson's appointment provided cover in case of a failure.[[127]](#cite_note-128)

In August 1963, Johnson was touched by a Senate scandal when [Bobby Baker](/wiki/Bobby_Baker "Bobby Baker"), the Secretary to the Majority Leader of the Senate and a protégé of Johnson's, came under investigation by the [Senate Rules Committee](/wiki/Senate_Rules_Committee "Senate Rules Committee") for alleged bribery and financial malfeasance. One witness alleged that Baker arranged for the witness to give kickbacks for the Vice President. Baker resigned in October, and the investigation did not expand to Johnson. The negative publicity, however, fed rumors in Washington circles that Kennedy was planning on dropping Johnson from the Democratic ticket in the [1964 presidential election](/wiki/1964_United_States_presidential_election "1964 United States presidential election"). However, on October 31, 1963, a reporter asked if he intended and expected to retain Johnson on the ticket. Kennedy replied, "Yes to both those questions."[[128]](#cite_note-129) There is little doubt that Robert Kennedy and Johnson hated each other,[[129]](#cite_note-130) yet John and Robert Kennedy agreed that dropping Johnson from the ticket could produce heavy losses in the South.[[130]](#cite_note-131)[[131]](#cite_note-132)

## Presidency (1963–1969)

Main article: [Presidency of Lyndon B. Johnson](/wiki/Presidency_of_Lyndon_B._Johnson "Presidency of Lyndon B. Johnson")

### Succession

Main article: [First inauguration of Lyndon B. Johnson](/wiki/First_inauguration_of_Lyndon_B._Johnson "First inauguration of Lyndon B. Johnson")

Further information: [Assassination of John F. Kennedy](/wiki/Assassination_of_John_F._Kennedy "Assassination of John F. Kennedy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/LBJOathOfOffice1963.jpg/330px-LBJOathOfOffice1963.jpg)](/wiki/File:LBJOathOfOffice1963.jpg)

Johnson is sworn in as [president](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") aboard [Air Force One](/wiki/Air_Force_One "Air Force One") at [Dallas Love Field](/wiki/Dallas_Love_Field "Dallas Love Field") two hours and eight minutes following [Kennedy's assassination](/wiki/Assassination_of_John_F._Kennedy "Assassination of John F. Kennedy") as [Mrs. Johnson](/wiki/Lady_Bird_Johnson "Lady Bird Johnson") and [Mrs. Kennedy](/wiki/Jacqueline_Kennedy_Onassis "Jacqueline Kennedy Onassis") look on.

President Kennedy was [assassinated](/wiki/Assassination_of_John_F._Kennedy "Assassination of John F. Kennedy") on November 22, 1963, in [Dallas](/wiki/Dallas "Dallas"), Texas.[[132]](#cite_note-dallek4951-133) Later that day, Johnson took the [presidential oath of office](/wiki/Oath_of_office_of_the_President_of_the_United_States "Oath of office of the President of the United States") aboard *[Air Force One](/wiki/Air_Force_One "Air Force One")*.[[133]](#cite_note-KennethTWalsh-134) [Cecil Stoughton](/wiki/Cecil_Stoughton "Cecil Stoughton")'s iconic photograph of Johnson taking the oath of office as Mrs. Kennedy looks on is the most famous photo ever taken aboard a presidential aircraft.[[134]](#cite_note-AirForceOne-135)[[133]](#cite_note-KennethTWalsh-134) Johnson was sworn in by District Court judge [Sarah T. Hughes](/wiki/Sarah_T._Hughes "Sarah T. Hughes") and is to date the only president in U.S. history to be sworn in by a woman.[[135]](#cite_note-136)[[136]](#cite_note-137)

Johnson was convinced of the need to make an immediate show of transition of power after the assassination to provide stability to a grieving nation.[[137]](#cite_note-138) He and the [Secret Service](/wiki/United_States_Secret_Service "United States Secret Service"), not knowing whether the assassin [acted alone](/wiki/Lone_wolf_(terrorism) "Lone wolf (terrorism)") or as part of a [broader conspiracy](/wiki/Conspiracy_(criminal) "Conspiracy (criminal)"), felt compelled to return rapidly to Washington, D.C.; this was greeted by some with assertions that he was in too much haste to assume power.[[132]](#cite_note-dallek4951-133) In response to the public demand for answers and the growing number of [conspiracy theories](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_assassination_conspiracy_theories "John F. Kennedy assassination conspiracy theories"), Johnson established a commission headed by Chief Justice [Earl Warren](/wiki/Earl_Warren "Earl Warren"), known as the [Warren Commission](/wiki/Warren_Commission "Warren Commission"), to investigate Kennedy's assassination.[[138]](#cite_note-139) The commission conducted research and hearings and unanimously concluded that [Lee Harvey Oswald](/wiki/Lee_Harvey_Oswald "Lee Harvey Oswald") acted alone in the assassination.[[139]](#cite_note-140) Although Johnson publicly supported the findings of the Warren Commission, privately he expressed skepticism of its conclusions. In 1967 he told his advisor [W. Marvin Watson](/wiki/W._Marvin_Watson "W. Marvin Watson") that he was convinced the CIA was in some way involved in the assassination,[[140]](#cite_note-141) and shortly before his death, he told his speechwriter [Leo Janos](/wiki/Leo_Janos "Leo Janos") "I never believed that Oswald acted alone, although I can accept that he pulled the trigger".[[141]](#cite_note-LastDays-142)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Cabinet_Meeting_July_1965.jpg/330px-Cabinet_Meeting_July_1965.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cabinet_Meeting_July_1965.jpg)

Johnson at a July 1965 Cabinet meeting

When Johnson assumed office, he asked the existing Cabinet to remain in place.[[142]](#cite_note-143) Despite his notoriously poor relationship with Johnson, Robert F. Kennedy stayed on as Attorney General until September 1964, when he resigned to [run for the U.S. Senate](/wiki/1964_United_States_Senate_election_in_New_York "1964 United States Senate election in New York").[[143]](#cite_note-144) Four of the Kennedy cabinet members Johnson inherited—Secretary of State [Dean Rusk](/wiki/Dean_Rusk "Dean Rusk"), Secretary of the Interior [Stewart Udall](/wiki/Stewart_Udall "Stewart Udall"), Secretary of Agriculture [Orville L. Freeman](/wiki/Orville_L._Freeman "Orville L. Freeman"), and Secretary of Labor [W. Willard Wirtz](/wiki/W._Willard_Wirtz "W. Willard Wirtz")—served until the end of Johnson's presidency.[[144]](#cite_note-145) Other Kennedy holdovers, including Secretary of Defense [Robert McNamara](/wiki/Robert_McNamara "Robert McNamara"), left office during Johnson's tenure. Although Johnson had no official chief of staff, [Walter Jenkins](/wiki/Walter_Jenkins "Walter Jenkins") presided over daily operations at the White House. [George Reedy](/wiki/George_Reedy "George Reedy"), who was Johnson's second-longest-serving aide, assumed the post of [press secretary](/wiki/White_House_Press_Secretary "White House Press Secretary") when John F. Kennedy's own [Pierre Salinger](/wiki/Pierre_Salinger "Pierre Salinger") left that post in March 1964.[[145]](#cite_note-146) [Horace Busby](/wiki/Horace_Busby "Horace Busby") served primarily as a speechwriter and political analyst.[[146]](#cite_note-147) [Bill Moyers](/wiki/Bill_Moyers "Bill Moyers") handled scheduling and speechwriting part-time.[[147]](#cite_note-148)

### Taxation and budget

The new president thought it advantageous to quickly pursue one of Kennedy's primary legislative goals—a [tax cut](/wiki/Tax_cut "Tax cut"). Johnson worked closely with Senator [Harry F. Byrd](/wiki/Harry_F._Byrd "Harry F. Byrd") of [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia") to negotiate a reduction in the budget below $100 billion in exchange for what became overwhelming Senate approval of the [Revenue Act of 1964](/wiki/Revenue_Act_of_1964 "Revenue Act of 1964"). The act cut individual income tax rates across the board by approximately 20 percent, cut the top marginal tax rate from 91 to 70 percent, and slightly reduced corporate tax rates.[[148]](#cite_note-149) Passage of the long-stalled tax cut facilitated efforts to move ahead on civil rights legislation.[[149]](#cite_note-150) Despite a period of strong economic growth, heavy spending on the Vietnam War and on domestic programs contributed to a rising budget deficit, as well as a period of [inflation](/wiki/Inflation "Inflation") that would continue into the 1970s.[[150]](#cite_note-151) To counter this, Johnson reluctantly signed a second tax bill, the [Revenue and Expenditure Control Act of 1968](/wiki/Revenue_and_Expenditure_Control_Act_of_1968 "Revenue and Expenditure Control Act of 1968"), which included a mix of tax increases and spending cuts, producing a budget surplus.[[151]](#cite_note-152)[[152]](#cite_note-153)

### Civil Rights Act of 1964

Main article: [Civil Rights Act of 1964](/wiki/Civil_Rights_Act_of_1964 "Civil Rights Act of 1964")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Lyndon_Johnson_meeting_with_civil_rights_leaders.jpg/250px-Lyndon_Johnson_meeting_with_civil_rights_leaders.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lyndon_Johnson_meeting_with_civil_rights_leaders.jpg)

Johnson meeting with civil rights leaders [Martin Luther King Jr.](/wiki/Martin_Luther_King_Jr. "Martin Luther King Jr.") (left), [Whitney Young](/wiki/Whitney_Young "Whitney Young"), and [James Farmer](/wiki/James_Farmer "James Farmer") in the [Oval Office](/wiki/Oval_Office "Oval Office") on January 18, 1964

On November 27, 1963, Johnson delivered his ["Let Us Continue" speech](/wiki/Let_Us_Continue "Let Us Continue") to Congress, saying that "No memorial oration or eulogy could more eloquently honor President Kennedy's memory than the earliest possible passage of the Civil Rights Bill for which he fought so long."[[153]](#cite_note-154) The wave of national grief following the assassination gave enormous momentum to Johnson's promise to carry out Kennedy's plans.[[154]](#cite_note-Walsh_2003_78-155)

Johnson asked Robert Kennedy to spearhead the undertaking on Capitol Hill. This provided adequate political cover for Johnson should the effort fail, but if it were successful, Johnson would receive ample credit.[[155]](#cite_note-156) Biographer Caro notes that the bill Kennedy had submitted was facing the same tactics that prevented the passage of civil rights bills in the past: Southern congressmen and senators used congressional procedure to prevent it from coming to a vote.[[156]](#cite_note-Caro,_Robert_p459-157) In particular, they held up all of the major bills Kennedy had proposed and that were considered urgent, especially the tax reform bill, to force the bill's supporters to pull it.[[156]](#cite_note-Caro,_Robert_p459-157) For Johnson's civil rights bill to reach the House floor for a vote, the president needed to find a way to circumvent Representative [Howard W. Smith](/wiki/Howard_W._Smith "Howard W. Smith"), the chairman of the [House Rules Committee](/wiki/United_States_House_Committee_on_Rules "United States House Committee on Rules"). Johnson used a [discharge petition](/wiki/Discharge_petition "Discharge petition") to force it onto the House floor.[[157]](#cite_note-Caro,_Robert_p462-158) Facing a growing threat that they would be bypassed, the Rules Committee approved the bill and moved it to the floor of the full House, which soon passed it by a vote of 290–110.[[158]](#cite_note-159) Johnson convinced Senate Majority Leader [Mike Mansfield](/wiki/Mike_Mansfield "Mike Mansfield") to put the House bill directly into consideration by the full Senate, bypassing the [Senate Judiciary Committee](/wiki/United_States_Senate_Committee_on_the_Judiciary "United States Senate Committee on the Judiciary") and its segregationist chairman [James Eastland](/wiki/James_Eastland "James Eastland").[[159]](#cite_note-160) Since bottling up the civil rights bill in a committee was no longer an option, the anti-civil rights senators were left with the [filibuster](/wiki/Filibuster_in_the_United_States_Senate "Filibuster in the United States Senate") as their only remaining tool. Overcoming the filibuster required the support of at least 20 Republicans, who were growing less supportive because their party was about to nominate for president a candidate who opposed the bill.[[160]](#cite_note-161) According to Caro, Johnson ultimately could convince Republican leader [Everett Dirksen](/wiki/Everett_Dirksen "Everett Dirksen") to support the bill that amassed the necessary Republican votes to overcome the filibuster in March 1964; the bill passed the Senate by a vote of 71–29.[[161]](#cite_note-162)[[162]](#cite_note-Schlesinger-Pages644-645-163)

Johnson signed the [Civil Rights Act of 1964](/wiki/Civil_Rights_Act_of_1964 "Civil Rights Act of 1964") into law on July 2.[[162]](#cite_note-Schlesinger-Pages644-645-163) The act outlawed [discrimination](/wiki/Discrimination "Discrimination") based on [race](/wiki/Race_(human_categorization) "Race (human categorization)"), [color](/wiki/Person_of_color "Person of color"), national origin, religion, or sex.[[163]](#cite_note-164) It prohibited racial segregation in [public accommodations](/wiki/Public_accommodations "Public accommodations") and [employment discrimination](/wiki/Employment_discrimination_law_in_the_United_States "Employment discrimination law in the United States"), and strengthened the federal government's power to investigate racial and gender employment discrimination.[[164]](#cite_note-165) The following evening, Johnson told aide [Bill Moyers](/wiki/Bill_Moyers "Bill Moyers"), "I think we may have lost the South for your lifetime – and mine", anticipating a backlash from Southern whites against Johnson's Democratic Party.[[165]](#cite_note-Kaiser2023-166)[[166]](#cite_note-167)

### Great Society

Main article: [Great Society](/wiki/Great_Society "Great Society")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/President_Lyndon_Johnson%2C_1964_Commencement_BL012297.jpg/250px-President_Lyndon_Johnson%2C_1964_Commencement_BL012297.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Lyndon_Johnson,_1964_Commencement_BL012297.jpg)

President Johnson formally presented his specific goals for the [Great Society](/wiki/Great_Society "Great Society") during a speech at the [University of Michigan](/wiki/University_of_Michigan "University of Michigan"), May 1964.

By early 1964, Johnson had begun to use the name "[Great Society](/wiki/Great_Society "Great Society")" to describe his domestic program.[[167]](#cite_note-168) Johnson's Great Society program encompassed movements of urban renewal, modern transportation, clean environment, anti-poverty, healthcare reform, crime control, and educational reform.[[168]](#cite_note-169) To ensure the passage of his programs, Johnson placed an unprecedented emphasis on relations with Congress.[[169]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson1996530–532-170)

### 1964 presidential election

Main articles: [Lyndon B. Johnson 1964 presidential campaign](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson_1964_presidential_campaign "Lyndon B. Johnson 1964 presidential campaign") and [1964 United States presidential election](/wiki/1964_United_States_presidential_election "1964 United States presidential election")

Further information: [1964 Democratic National Convention](/wiki/1964_Democratic_National_Convention "1964 Democratic National Convention")

In Spring 1964, Johnson was not optimistic about his prospects of being elected president.[[170]](#cite_note-171) A pivotal change took place in April when he assumed personal management of negotiations between the railroad brotherhood and the railroad industry over the issue of [featherbedding](/wiki/Featherbedding "Featherbedding"). Johnson emphasized to the parties the potential impact upon the economy of a strike. After considerable horse-trading, especially with the carriers who won promises from the president for greater freedom in setting rights and more liberal depreciation allowances from the [Internal Revenue Service](/wiki/Internal_Revenue_Service "Internal Revenue Service"), Johnson obtained an agreement. This substantially boosted his self-confidence and image.[[171]](#cite_note-172)

Robert F. Kennedy was widely considered an impeccable choice for Johnson's [vice presidential](/wiki/Vice_President_of_the_United_States "Vice President of the United States") running mate but Johnson and Kennedy had never liked each other, and Johnson, afraid that Kennedy would be credited with his election as president, consistently opposed the idea.[[172]](#cite_note-173) Kennedy was undecided about accepting an offer as Johnson's running mate, knowing that the prospect rankled Johnson. [Barry Goldwater](/wiki/Barry_Goldwater "Barry Goldwater")'s poor polling numbers was perceived as reducing the political significance of Johnson's selection of a running mate.[[173]](#cite_note-174) Hubert Humphrey's selection as vice president then became a foregone conclusion and was thought to strengthen Johnson in the [Midwest](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States") and industrial [Northeast](/wiki/Northeastern_United_States "Northeastern United States").[[174]](#cite_note-Dallek_1998,_p._157-175) Knowing the degree of frustration inherent in the office of vice president, Johnson put Humphrey through a gauntlet of interviews to guarantee his loyalty. Having made the decision, he kept the announcement from the press until the last moment to maximize media speculation and coverage.[[175]](#cite_note-176)

In preparation for the [Democratic convention](/wiki/1964_Democratic_National_Convention "1964 Democratic National Convention") in [Atlantic City](/wiki/Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey "Atlantic City, New Jersey"), Johnson requested the [FBI](/wiki/Federal_Bureau_of_Investigation "Federal Bureau of Investigation") send 30 agents to cover convention activities; the objective of the squad was to inform the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House") staff of any disruptive activities. The squad's focus narrowed upon the [Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party](/wiki/Mississippi_Freedom_Democratic_Party "Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party") (MFDP) delegation, which sought to displace the white segregationist delegation regularly selected in the state. The squad's activities included wiretaps of [Martin Luther King](/wiki/Martin_Luther_King "Martin Luther King")'s room and the [Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee](/wiki/Student_Nonviolent_Coordinating_Committee "Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee") (SNCC) and the [Congress of Racial Equality](/wiki/Congress_of_Racial_Equality "Congress of Racial Equality") (CORE). From beginning to end, the squad's assignment was carefully couched in terms of the monitoring of disruptive activities that might endanger the president and other high-ranking officials.[[176]](#cite_note-177)

Johnson was very concerned about potential political damage from media coverage of racial tensions exposed by a credentials fight between the MFDP and the segregationist delegation, and he assigned Humphrey to manage the problem.[[177]](#cite_note-178) The convention's Credentials Committee declared that two MFDP delegates in the delegation be seated as observers and agreed to "bar future delegations from states where any citizens are deprived of the right to vote because of their race or color".[[178]](#cite_note-179) The MFDP rejected the committee's ruling. The convention became the apparent personal triumph that Johnson craved, but a sense of betrayal caused by the marginalization of the MFDP would trigger disaffection with Johnson and the Democratic Party from the left; SNCC chairman [John Lewis](/wiki/John_Lewis "John Lewis") would call it a "turning point in the civil rights movement".[[179]](#cite_note-lewis-180)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/ElectoralCollege1964.svg/330px-ElectoralCollege1964.svg.png)](/wiki/File:ElectoralCollege1964.svg)

Results of the [1964 presidential election](/wiki/1964_United_States_presidential_election "1964 United States presidential election"). Johnson won 486 [electoral college](/wiki/United_States_Electoral_College "United States Electoral College") votes (61.1% of the popular vote) to [Barry Goldwater](/wiki/Barry_Goldwater "Barry Goldwater")'s 52.

Early in the 1964 presidential campaign, Barry Goldwater appeared to be a strong contender, with strong support from the South, which threatened Johnson's position as he had predicted in reaction to the passage of the Civil Rights Act. However, Goldwater lost momentum as his campaign progressed. On September 7, 1964, Johnson's campaign managers broadcast the "[Daisy ad](/wiki/Daisy_(television_commercial) "Daisy (television commercial)")": it portrayed a little girl picking petals from a [daisy](/wiki/Common_daisy "Common daisy"), followed by a countdown and explosion of a nuclear bomb. The message conveyed was that electing Goldwater risked a nuclear war. Goldwater's campaign message was best symbolized by the bumper sticker displayed by supporters claiming "In your heart, you know he's right". Opponents captured the spirit of Johnson's campaign with bumper stickers that said "In your heart, you know he might" and "In your guts, you know he's nuts".[[180]](#cite_note-181) CIA Director [William Colby](/wiki/William_Colby "William Colby") asserted that [Tracy Barnes](/wiki/Tracy_Barnes "Tracy Barnes") instructed the CIA to spy on the Goldwater campaign and the Republican National Committee to provide information to Johnson's campaign.[[181]](#cite_note-182) Johnson won the presidency by a landslide with 61.05 percent of the vote, making it the highest ever [share of the popular vote](/wiki/List_of_United_States_presidential_elections_by_popular_vote_margin "List of United States presidential elections by popular vote margin").[[182]](#cite_note-183) At the time, this was also the widest popular margin in the 20th century—more than 15.95 million votes—this was later surpassed by incumbent President Nixon's victory in [1972](/wiki/1972_United_States_presidential_election "1972 United States presidential election").[[183]](#cite_note-184) In the [Electoral College](/wiki/Electoral_College_(United_States) "Electoral College (United States)"), Johnson defeated Goldwater by a margin of 486 to 52. Johnson won 44 states, compared to Goldwater's six. Voters also gave Johnson the largest majorities in Congress since FDR's election in 1936—a Senate with a 68–32 majority and a House with a 295–140 Democratic margin.[[184]](#cite_note-185)

### Voting Rights Act

Main article: [Voting Rights Act of 1965](/wiki/Voting_Rights_Act_of_1965 "Voting Rights Act of 1965")

[![refer to caption](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Lyndon_Johnson_and_Martin_Luther_King%2C_Jr._-_Voting_Rights_Act.jpg/250px-Lyndon_Johnson_and_Martin_Luther_King%2C_Jr._-_Voting_Rights_Act.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lyndon_Johnson_and_Martin_Luther_King,_Jr._-_Voting_Rights_Act.jpg)

President Lyndon B. Johnson, [Martin Luther King Jr.](/wiki/Martin_Luther_King_Jr. "Martin Luther King Jr."), and [Rosa Parks](/wiki/Rosa_Parks "Rosa Parks") at the signing of the [Voting Rights Act](/wiki/Voting_Rights_Act "Voting Rights Act") on August 6, 1965

Soon after the 1964 election, civil rights organizations such as the [Southern Christian Leadership Conference](/wiki/Southern_Christian_Leadership_Conference "Southern Christian Leadership Conference") (SCLC) and the [Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee](/wiki/Student_Nonviolent_Coordinating_Committee "Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee") (SNCC) began a push for federal action to protect the [voting rights](/wiki/Voting_rights "Voting rights") of racial minorities.[[185]](#cite_note-eyes-186) On March 7, 1965, SCLC began the [Selma to Montgomery marches](/wiki/Selma_to_Montgomery_marches "Selma to Montgomery marches") in which Selma residents, on their third attempt on March 21, proceeded to march to Alabama's capital, [Montgomery](/wiki/Montgomery,_Alabama "Montgomery, Alabama"), to highlight voting rights issues and present Governor [George Wallace](/wiki/George_Wallace "George Wallace") with their grievances. On the first march, demonstrators were stopped by state and county police, who shot [tear gas](/wiki/Tear_gas "Tear gas") into the crowd and beat and trampled protesters. Televised footage of the scene, which became known as "Bloody Sunday", generated outrage across the country.[[186]](#cite_note-GaryMay2015-187) In response to the rapidly increasing political pressure upon him, Johnson decided to immediately send voting rights legislation to Congress, and to address the American people in a speech before a [Joint session of Congress](/wiki/Joint_session_of_the_United_States_Congress "Joint session of the United States Congress"). He began:

> I speak tonight for the dignity of man and the destiny of democracy. I urge every member of both parties, Americans of all religions and of all colors, from every section of this country, to join me in that cause. ... Rarely in any time does an issue lay bare the secret heart of America itself. Rarely are we met with a challenge, not to our growth or abundance, or our welfare or our security, but rather to the values and the purposes and the meaning of our beloved nation. The issue of equal rights for American Negroes is such an issue. And should we defeat every enemy, and should we double our wealth and conquer the stars, and still be unequal to this issue, then we will have failed as a people and as a nation. For, with a country as with a person, 'what is a man profited if he shall gain the whole world, and lose his own soul?'[[186]](#cite_note-GaryMay2015-187)[[187]](#cite_note-Dallek_(1998),_p.218-188)

Johnson and Dirksen established a strong bipartisan alliance in favor of the [Voting Rights Act of 1965](/wiki/Voting_Rights_Act_of_1965 "Voting Rights Act of 1965"), precluding the possibility of a Senate filibuster defeating the bill. In August 1965, the House approved the bill by a vote of 333 to 85, and Senate passed the bill by a vote of 79 to 18.[[188]](#cite_note-189) The landmark legislation outlawed discrimination in voting, thus allowing millions of Southern blacks to vote for the first time. The results were significant; between the years of 1968 and 1980, the number of Southern black elected state and federal officeholders nearly doubled.[[187]](#cite_note-Dallek_(1998),_p.218-188) In Mississippi, the voter registration rate of African Americans rose from 6.7 percent to 59.8 percent between 1964 and 1967, a reflection of a broader increase in African-American voter registration rates.[[189]](#cite_note-190)

After the murder of civil rights worker [Viola Liuzzo](/wiki/Viola_Liuzzo "Viola Liuzzo"), Johnson went on television to announce the arrest of four [Ku Klux Klans](/wiki/Ku_Klux_Klan "Ku Klux Klan") men implicated in her death. He angrily denounced the Klan as a "hooded society of bigots," and warned them to "return to a decent society before it's too late". Johnson was the first president to arrest and prosecute members of the Klan since [Ulysses S. Grant](/wiki/Ulysses_S._Grant "Ulysses S. Grant").[[b]](#cite_note-191)[[190]](#cite_note-192) He turned to themes of Christian redemption to push for civil rights, mobilizing support from churches.[[191]](#cite_note-193) At the [Howard University](/wiki/Howard_University "Howard University") commencement address on June 4, 1965, he said that both the government and the nation needed to help achieve these goals: "To shatter forever not only the barriers of law and public practice but the walls which bound the condition of many by the color of his skin. To dissolve, as best we can, the antique enmities of the heart which diminish the holder, divide the great democracy, and do wrong—great wrong ‍—‌ to the children of God ..."[[192]](#cite_note-194)

### Fair Housing Act

See also: [Civil Rights Act of 1968](/wiki/Civil_Rights_Act_of_1968 "Civil Rights Act of 1968")
