The [Fair Housing Act](/wiki/Fair_Housing_Act "Fair Housing Act"), a component of the [Civil Rights Act of 1968](/wiki/Civil_Rights_Act_of_1968 "Civil Rights Act of 1968"), outlawed several forms of [housing discrimination](/wiki/Housing_discrimination "Housing discrimination") and effectively allowed many African Americans to move to the suburbs.[[193]](#cite_note-mfletcher1-195) Johnson submitted a bill to Congress in April 1966 that barred house owners from refusing to enter into agreements on the basis of race; the bill immediately garnered opposition from many of the Northerners who had supported the last two major civil rights bills.[[194]](#cite_note-196) Though a version of the bill passed the House, it failed to win Senate approval, marking Johnson's first major legislative defeat.[[195]](#cite_note-197) The law gained new impetus after the April 4, 1968, [assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.](/wiki/Assassination_of_Martin_Luther_King_Jr. "Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr."), and the [civil unrest](/wiki/King_assassination_riots "King assassination riots") across the country that followed.[[196]](#cite_note-Kotz2005P417-198) The bill passed Congress on April 10 and was quickly signed into law by Johnson.[[196]](#cite_note-Kotz2005P417-198)[[197]](#cite_note-199)

### War on Poverty

Main article: [War on Poverty](/wiki/War_on_Poverty "War on Poverty")

Further information: [Presidency of Lyndon B. Johnson § War on Poverty](/wiki/Presidency_of_Lyndon_B._Johnson#War_on_Poverty "Presidency of Lyndon B. Johnson")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/President_Johnson_poverty_tour.jpg/250px-President_Johnson_poverty_tour.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Johnson_poverty_tour.jpg)

President Johnson's poverty tour in 1964

In his 1964 State of the Union Address Johnson stated, "this administration today, here and now, declares an unconditional [war on poverty](/wiki/War_on_Poverty "War on Poverty") in America. Our aim is not only to relieve the symptoms of [poverty](/wiki/Poverty_in_the_United_States "Poverty in the United States") but to cure it–and above all, to prevent it."[[198]](#cite_note-200) During the Johnson administration, national poverty declined significantly, with the percentage of Americans living below the poverty line dropping from 23 to 12 percent.[[3]](#cite_note-histeval-4)

In August 1964, Johnson signed the [Economic Opportunity Act of 1964](/wiki/Economic_Opportunity_Act_of_1964 "Economic Opportunity Act of 1964"), which would create the [Office of Economic Opportunity](/wiki/Office_of_Economic_Opportunity "Office of Economic Opportunity") (OEO) to oversee local [Community Action Agencies](/wiki/Community_Action_Agencies "Community Action Agencies") (CAA) charged with dispensing aid to those in poverty.[[199]](#cite_note-201) Each CAA was required to have "maximum feasible participation" from local residents, who would design and operate [anti-poverty](/wiki/Anti-poverty "Anti-poverty") programs unique to their communities' needs.[[200]](#cite_note-202) This was threatening to local political leaders who saw CAAs as alternative [power structures](/wiki/Power_structure "Power structure") in their own communities, funded and encouraged by the OEO.[[201]](#cite_note-The_Liberal_Hour-203)[[202]](#cite_note-204)[[203]](#cite_note-lbj_russell_cap_1966_06_02_miller-205) In 1967, the Green Amendment gave city governments the right to decide which entity would be the official CAA for their community. The net result was a halt to the [citizen participation](/wiki/Citizen_participation "Citizen participation") reform movement.[[204]](#cite_note-206)[[205]](#cite_note-207)

The Economic Opportunity Act created the [Job Corps](/wiki/Job_Corps "Job Corps") and [Volunteers in Service to America](/wiki/AmeriCorps_VISTA "AmeriCorps VISTA") (VISTA), a domestic version of the [Peace Corps](/wiki/Peace_Corps "Peace Corps").[[206]](#cite_note-208) Modeled after the [Civilian Conservation Corps](/wiki/Civilian_Conservation_Corps "Civilian Conservation Corps") (CCC), Job Corps was a [residential education](/wiki/Residential_education "Residential education") and job-training program that provided academic and [vocational skills](/wiki/Vocational_education "Vocational education") to low-income at-risk young people.[[207]](#cite_note-Economic_Opportunity_Act-209)[[208]](#cite_note-210) VISTA deployed volunteers on community projects across the nation to address issues such as [illiteracy](/wiki/Illiteracy "Illiteracy"), inadequate housing, and poor health.[[207]](#cite_note-Economic_Opportunity_Act-209) By the end of 1965, 2,000 volunteers had signed on.[[209]](#cite_note-211) Congress also agreed to [Upward Bound](/wiki/Upward_Bound "Upward Bound"), a program that trained low-income students in the skills they needed for college.[[210]](#cite_note-212) The act reflected Johnson's belief that the government could best help the impoverished by providing them with economic opportunities.[[211]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson1996535,_538–539-213) Johnson convinced Congress to approve the [Food Stamp Act of 1964](/wiki/Food_Stamp_Act_of_1964 "Food Stamp Act of 1964"), which made permanent the [food stamp pilot programs](/wiki/Food_Stamp_Program "Food Stamp Program") that had been initiated by President Kennedy.[[212]](#cite_note-214)

To combat [homelessness](/wiki/Homelessness "Homelessness"), Johnson signed the [Housing and Urban Development Act](/wiki/Housing_and_Urban_Development_Act "Housing and Urban Development Act") of 1965, which provided rent subsidies for the elderly and disabled, construction of 240,000 housing units, and $3 billion for [urban renewal](/wiki/Urban_renewal "Urban renewal"). In September 1965, Johnson would go on to sign legislation that would establish the [Department of Housing and Urban Development](/wiki/Department_of_Housing_and_Urban_Development "Department of Housing and Urban Development") (HUD), to oversee the newly funded housing programs. Providing an additional $1.1 billion for roads, health clinics, and other public works was the [Appalachian Regional Development Act](/wiki/Appalachian_Regional_Development_Act "Appalachian Regional Development Act"), a measure to improve living standards in [Appalachia](/wiki/Appalachia "Appalachia").[[213]](#cite_note-215)[[214]](#cite_note-216)

Johnson took an additional step in the War on Poverty with an urban renewal effort, the "Demonstration Cities Program". To be eligible, a city was required to demonstrate its readiness to "arrest blight and [decay](/wiki/Urban_decay "Urban decay") and make a substantial impact on the development of its entire city". Johnson requested an investment of $400 million per year totaling $2.4 billion. In late 1966, Congress passed a substantially reduced program costing $900 million, which Johnson later called the [Model Cities Program](/wiki/Model_Cities_Program "Model Cities Program"). *The New York Times* wrote 22 years later that the program was largely a failure.[[215]](#cite_note-217)

### Healthcare reform

Further information: [Presidency of Lyndon B. Johnson § Medicare and Medicaid](/wiki/Presidency_of_Lyndon_B._Johnson#Medicare_and_Medicaid "Presidency of Lyndon B. Johnson")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Lyndon_Johnson_signing_Medicare_bill%2C_with_Harry_Truman%2C_July_30%2C_1965.jpg/250px-Lyndon_Johnson_signing_Medicare_bill%2C_with_Harry_Truman%2C_July_30%2C_1965.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lyndon_Johnson_signing_Medicare_bill,_with_Harry_Truman,_July_30,_1965.jpg)

Former president [Truman](/wiki/Harry_S._Truman "Harry S. Truman") and wife [Bess](/wiki/Bess_Truman "Bess Truman") at Johnson's signing of the [Medicare Bill](/wiki/Medicare_Bill "Medicare Bill") in 1965, as [Lady Bird](/wiki/Lady_Bird_Johnson "Lady Bird Johnson") and [Hubert Humphrey](/wiki/Hubert_Humphrey "Hubert Humphrey") look on

Johnson's initial effort to improve [healthcare](/wiki/Healthcare "Healthcare") was the creation of The Commission on Heart Disease, Cancer, and Strokes (HDCS). These diseases accounted for 71 percent of the nation's deaths in 1962.[[216]](#cite_note-218)

In 1965, Johnson turned his focus to [hospital insurance](/wiki/Health_insurance "Health insurance") for the aged under [Social Security](/wiki/Social_Security_(United_States) "Social Security (United States)").[[217]](#cite_note-219) The key player in initiating this program, named [Medicare](/wiki/Medicare_(United_States) "Medicare (United States)"), was Representative [Wilbur Mills](/wiki/Wilbur_Mills "Wilbur Mills"), Chairman of the [House Ways and Means Committee](/wiki/House_Ways_and_Means_Committee "House Ways and Means Committee"). To reduce Republican opposition, Mills suggested that Medicare be fashioned as three layers: hospital insurance under Social Security; a voluntary insurance program for doctor visits; and an expanded medical welfare program for the poor administered through the states, known as [Medicaid](/wiki/Medicaid "Medicaid").[[218]](#cite_note-220) The bill passed the House by a margin of 110 votes on April 8. The effort in the Senate was considerably more complicated, but the Medicare bill passed Congress on July 28.[[219]](#cite_note-221) Medicare now covers tens of millions of Americans.[[220]](#cite_note-222)

### Immigration

See also: [Immigration and Nationality Act of 1965](/wiki/Immigration_and_Nationality_Act_of_1965 "Immigration and Nationality Act of 1965")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/President_Lyndon_B._Johnson_Signing_of_the_Immigration_Act_of_1965_%2802%29_-_restoration1.jpg/250px-President_Lyndon_B._Johnson_Signing_of_the_Immigration_Act_of_1965_%2802%29_-_restoration1.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Lyndon_B._Johnson_Signing_of_the_Immigration_Act_of_1965_(02)_-_restoration1.jpg)

President Johnson signs the [Immigration and Nationality Act of 1965](/wiki/Immigration_and_Nationality_Act_of_1965 "Immigration and Nationality Act of 1965") as U.S. Senators [Edward Kennedy](/wiki/Edward_Kennedy "Edward Kennedy") and [Robert F. Kennedy](/wiki/Robert_F._Kennedy "Robert F. Kennedy"), and others look on.

The sweeping [Immigration and Nationality Act of 1965](/wiki/Immigration_and_Nationality_Act_of_1965 "Immigration and Nationality Act of 1965") reformed the country's immigration system and repealed the [National Origins Formula](/wiki/National_Origins_Formula "National Origins Formula"), which had restricted emigration from countries outside of Western Europe and the Western Hemisphere. The annual rate of inflow doubled between 1965 and 1970, and doubled again by 1990, with dramatic increases from [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia") and [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America"), including Mexico.[[62]](#cite_note-'70s-63) Scholars give Johnson little credit for the law, which was not one of his priorities; he had supported the [McCarren–Walter Act](/wiki/Immigration_and_Nationality_Act_of_1952 "Immigration and Nationality Act of 1952") of 1952, which proved unpopular with reformers.[[221]](#cite_note-223)

### Federal funding for education

See also: [Elementary and Secondary Education Act](/wiki/Elementary_and_Secondary_Education_Act "Elementary and Secondary Education Act")

Further information: [Presidency of Lyndon B. Johnson § Education](/wiki/Presidency_of_Lyndon_B._Johnson#Education "Presidency of Lyndon B. Johnson")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Lady_Bird_Johnson_Visiting_a_Classroom_for_Project_Head_Start_1966.gif/250px-Lady_Bird_Johnson_Visiting_a_Classroom_for_Project_Head_Start_1966.gif)](/wiki/File:Lady_Bird_Johnson_Visiting_a_Classroom_for_Project_Head_Start_1966.gif)

First Lady [Lady Bird Johnson](/wiki/Lady_Bird_Johnson "Lady Bird Johnson") visits a [Head Start](/wiki/Head_Start_(program) "Head Start (program)") class, 1966.

Johnson, whose own ticket out of poverty was a public education in Texas, believed that education was an essential component of the [American dream](/wiki/American_dream "American dream"), especially for minorities.[[222]](#cite_note-224) He made education the top priority of the Great Society agenda, with an emphasis on helping poor children. Buoyed by his landslide victory in the 1964 election, in early 1965 Johnson proposed the [Elementary and Secondary Education Act](/wiki/Elementary_and_Secondary_Education_Act "Elementary and Secondary Education Act") (ESEA), which would double federal spending on education from $4 billion to $8 billion.[[223]](#cite_note-225) The bill quickly passed both houses of Congress by wide margins.[[224]](#cite_note-226) ESEA increased funding to all school districts, but directed more money going to districts that had large proportions of students from poor families.[[225]](#cite_note-227)

Although ESEA solidified Johnson's support among K–12 teachers' unions, neither the Higher Education Act nor the new endowments mollified the college professors and students growing increasingly uneasy with the war in Vietnam.[[226]](#cite_note-228)[[227]](#cite_note-229) Johnson's second major education program was the [Higher Education Act of 1965](/wiki/Higher_Education_Act_of_1965 "Higher Education Act of 1965"), which increased federal money given to universities, created scholarships, gave low-interest loans to students, and established a [Teacher Corps](/wiki/Teacher_Corps "Teacher Corps").[[228]](#cite_note-230) Johnson also established [Head Start](/wiki/Head_Start_(program) "Head Start (program)"), an [early education](/wiki/Early_education "Early education") program to help prepare children from disadvantaged families for success in public schools.[[229]](#cite_note-231) In 1967, Johnson signed the [Public Broadcasting Act](/wiki/Public_Broadcasting_Act_of_1967 "Public Broadcasting Act of 1967") to create educational television programs to supplement broadcast networks.[[230]](#cite_note-clarkcq1-232)

In 1965, Johnson set up the [National Endowment for the Humanities](/wiki/National_Endowment_for_the_Humanities "National Endowment for the Humanities") and the [National Endowment for the Arts](/wiki/National_Endowment_for_the_Arts "National Endowment for the Arts"), to support the study of literature, history, and law, and arts such as music, painting, and sculpture.[[231]](#cite_note-233)

### Transportation

Further information: [Presidency of Lyndon B. Johnson § Transportation](/wiki/Presidency_of_Lyndon_B._Johnson#Transportation "Presidency of Lyndon B. Johnson")

In March 1966, Johnson sent to Congress a transportation message which included the creation of a new [Transportation Department](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Transportation "United States Department of Transportation"), comprising the Commerce Department's Office of Transportation, the Bureau of Public Roads, the [Federal Aviation Agency](/wiki/Federal_Aviation_Agency "Federal Aviation Agency"), the [Coast Guard](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard "United States Coast Guard"), the Maritime Administration, the Civil Aeronautics Board, and the [Interstate Commerce Commission](/wiki/Interstate_Commerce_Commission "Interstate Commerce Commission"). The bill passed the Senate after some negotiation over navigation projects; in the House, passage required negotiation over maritime interests and the bill was signed October 15, 1965.[[232]](#cite_note-234)

### Environment

Further information: [Presidency of Lyndon B. Johnson § Environment](/wiki/Presidency_of_Lyndon_B._Johnson#Environment "Presidency of Lyndon B. Johnson")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Clean_Air_Act_Signing_edited_cropped.jpg/250px-Clean_Air_Act_Signing_edited_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Clean_Air_Act_Signing_edited_cropped.jpg)

Johnson signing the Clean Air Act

During his tenure as president, Johnson signed over 300 [conservation](/wiki/Conservation_movement "Conservation movement") measures into law, forming the legal basis of the modern environmental movement.[[233]](#cite_note-235) He signed into law the [Clean Air Act of 1963](/wiki/Clean_Air_Act_(United_States) "Clean Air Act (United States)"), which had been proposed by President Kennedy. The Clean Air Act set [emission standards](/wiki/United_States_emission_standards "United States emission standards") for stationary emitters of air pollutants and directed federal funding to [air quality](/wiki/Air_quality "Air quality") research.[[234]](#cite_note-236) In 1965, the act was amended by the [Motor Vehicle Air Pollution Control Act](/wiki/Motor_Vehicle_Air_Pollution_Control_Act "Motor Vehicle Air Pollution Control Act"), which directed the federal government to establish and enforce national [standards for controlling the emission of pollutants](/wiki/Automobile_emissions_control "Automobile emissions control") from new motor vehicles and engines.[[235]](#cite_note-237) In September 1964, Johnson signed the [Wilderness Act](/wiki/Wilderness_Act "Wilderness Act"), which established the [National Wilderness Preservation System](/wiki/National_Wilderness_Preservation_System "National Wilderness Preservation System") (preserving 9.1 million acres of [forestland](/wiki/Forestland "Forestland") from industrial development),[[236]](#cite_note-238)[[237]](#cite_note-239) and signed a law establishing the [Land and Water Conservation Fund](/wiki/Land_and_Water_Conservation_Fund "Land and Water Conservation Fund"), which aids the purchase of land used for federal and state parks.[[238]](#cite_note-240)[[239]](#cite_note-241)

In 1965, First Lady [Lady Bird Johnson](/wiki/Lady_Bird_Johnson "Lady Bird Johnson") took the lead in calling for passage of the [Highway Beautification Act](/wiki/Highway_Beautification_Act "Highway Beautification Act").[[240]](#cite_note-242) The act called for control of [outdoor advertising](/wiki/Outdoor_advertising "Outdoor advertising"), including removal of certain types of signs, along the nation's growing [Interstate Highway System](/wiki/Interstate_Highway_System "Interstate Highway System") and the existing [federal-aid primary highway system](/wiki/Federal-aid_primary_highway_system "Federal-aid primary highway system"). It also required certain [junkyards](/wiki/Junkyards "Junkyards") along Interstate or primary highways to be removed or screened and encouraged scenic enhancement and roadside development.[[241]](#cite_note-243)

### Gun control

Though Johnson had already introduced a [gun control](/wiki/Gun_control "Gun control") bill on June 6, 1968, after the assassination of Robert Kennedy, [Lady Bird Johnson](/wiki/Lady_Bird_Johnson "Lady Bird Johnson")'s press secretary Liz Carpenter, in a memo to the president, worried that the country had been "brainwashed by high drama," and that Johnson "need[ed] some quick dramatic actions" that addressed "the issue of violence." In October, Johnson signed the [Gun Control Act of 1968](/wiki/Gun_Control_Act_of_1968 "Gun Control Act of 1968"), but did not invoke the memory of Robert Kennedy as he had so often done with his brother—an omission historian [Jeff Shesol](/wiki/Jeff_Shesol "Jeff Shesol") has argued was motivated by Johnson's longstanding contempt for Robert.[[242]](#cite_note-244) The measure prohibited convicted felons, drug users, and the mentally ill from purchasing handguns and raised record-keeping and licensing requirements.[[243]](#cite_note-245) It also banned [mail order](/wiki/Mail_order "Mail order") sales of rifles and shotguns.[[244]](#cite_note-246)

### Urban riots

Main article: [Ghetto riots (1964–1969)](/wiki/Ghetto_riots_(1964%E2%80%931969) "Ghetto riots (1964–1969)")

Further information: [Long, hot summer of 1967](/wiki/Long,_hot_summer_of_1967 "Long, hot summer of 1967") and [King assassination riots](/wiki/King_assassination_riots "King assassination riots")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/40th_in_Watts.jpg/250px-40th_in_Watts.jpg)](/wiki/File:40th_in_Watts.jpg)

Soldiers direct traffic away from an area of [South Central Los Angeles](/wiki/South_Central_Los_Angeles "South Central Los Angeles") burning during the [1965 Watts riots](/wiki/Watts_Riots "Watts Riots").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Newark_Riots_1967_%28305517077%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Newark_Riots_1967_%28305517077%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Newark_Riots_1967_(305517077)_(cropped).jpg)

[1967 Newark riots](/wiki/1967_Newark_riots "1967 Newark riots")

The nation experienced a series of "long hot summers" of [civil unrest](/wiki/Ghetto_riots_(1964%E2%80%931969) "Ghetto riots (1964–1969)") during the Johnson years. They started with the [Harlem riots](/wiki/Harlem_Riot_of_1964 "Harlem Riot of 1964") in 1964, and the [Watts district](/wiki/Watts_Riots "Watts Riots") of Los Angeles in 1965. The momentum for the advancement of civil rights came to a sudden halt following the riots in Watts. After 34 people were killed and $35 million (equivalent to $357.58 million in 2025) in property was damaged, the public feared an expansion of the violence to other cities, and so the appetite for additional programs in Johnson's agenda was lost.[[245]](#cite_note-247)[[246]](#cite_note-248)

In what is known as the "[Long hot summer of 1967](/wiki/Long_hot_summer_of_1967 "Long hot summer of 1967")", more than 150 riots erupted across the United States.[[247]](#cite_note-249) The *[Boston Globe](/wiki/Boston_Globe "Boston Globe")* called it "a revolution of black Americans against white Americans, a violent petition for the redress of long-standing grievances."[[248]](#cite_note-Dallek_1998,_p._412-250) The [Newark riots](/wiki/1967_Newark_riots "1967 Newark riots") left 26 dead and 1,500 injured.[[248]](#cite_note-Dallek_1998,_p._412-250) The [Detroit riot](/wiki/1967_Detroit_riot "1967 Detroit riot") resulted in 43 deaths, 2250 injuries, 4,000 arrests, and millions of dollars' worth of property damage. Governor [George Romney](/wiki/George_W._Romney "George W. Romney") sent in 7,400 [national guard troops](/wiki/National_Guard_(United_States) "National Guard (United States)") to quell [fire bombings](/wiki/Arson "Arson"), [looting](/wiki/Looting "Looting"), and attacks on businesses and police. Johnson finally sent in federal troops with [tanks](/wiki/Tanks "Tanks") and [machine guns](/wiki/Machine_guns "Machine guns").[[249]](#cite_note-LHS67-251) At an August 2, 1967, cabinet meeting, Attorney General [Ramsey Clark](/wiki/Ramsey_Clark "Ramsey Clark") warned that untrained and undisciplined local police forces and National Guardsmen might trigger a "[guerrilla war](/wiki/Guerrilla_war "Guerrilla war") in the streets,"[[250]](#cite_note-252)[[251]](#cite_note-253) as evidenced by the climate of [sniper](/wiki/Sniper "Sniper") fire in Newark and Detroit. Snipers created a dangerous situation for both law enforcement and civilians, with shooters often targeting from rooftops and other concealed locations.[[252]](#cite_note-254)[[253]](#cite_note-255)

Johnson's popularity plummeted as a massive white political backlash took shape, reinforcing the sense Johnson had lost control of the streets of major cities and his own party.[[254]](#cite_note-256) The president created the 11-member [Kerner Commission](/wiki/Kerner_Commission "Kerner Commission") to study the causes behind the recurring outbreaks of urban civil disorder, headed by Illinois Governor [Otto Kerner](/wiki/Otto_Kerner_Jr. "Otto Kerner Jr.").[[62]](#cite_note-'70s-63) The commission's 1968 report suggested legislative measures to promote racial integration and alleviate poverty and concluded that the nation was "moving toward two societies, one black, one white—separate and unequal."[[255]](#cite_note-kernerreport-257) According to Press Secretary [George Christian](/wiki/George_Christian_(journalist) "George Christian (journalist)"), Johnson was unsurprised by the riots, saying: "What did you expect? ... When you put your foot on a man's neck and hold him down for three hundred years, and then you let him up, what's he going to do? He's going to knock your block off."[[256]](#cite_note-Kotz2005P418-258)

### Backlash against Johnson (1966–1967)

In 1966, the press sensed a [credibility gap](/wiki/Credibility_gap "Credibility gap") between what Johnson was saying in press conferences and what was happening in the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War"), which led to less favorable coverage of Johnson and his administration.[[257]](#cite_note-american_chronicle-259)

By the end of 1966, Democratic Governor [Warren E. Hearnes](/wiki/Warren_E._Hearnes "Warren E. Hearnes") of Missouri warned that Johnson would lose the state by 100,000 votes, despite winning by a margin of 500,000 in 1964. "Frustration over Vietnam; too much federal spending and ... taxation; no great public support for your Great Society programs; and ... public disenchantment with the civil rights programs "had eroded the President's standing, the governor reported.[[258]](#cite_note-260) There were bright spots; in January 1967, Johnson boasted that wages were the highest in history, unemployment was at a 13-year low, and corporate profits and farm incomes were greater than ever; a 4.5 percent jump in consumer prices was worrisome, as was the rise in interest rates. Johnson asked for a temporary 6 percent surcharge in [income taxes](/wiki/Income_tax "Income tax") to cover the mounting deficit caused by increased spending. Johnson's [approval ratings](/wiki/Approval_rating "Approval rating") stayed below 50 percent and, in December 1966, his disapproval rating surpassed his approval rating. In January 1967, the number of his strong supporters had plunged to 16 percent, from 25 percent four months before.

Asked to explain his diminished popularity, Johnson said, "I am a dominating personality, and when I get things done I don't always please all the people."[[259]](#cite_note-261) Johnson also blamed the press, saying they showed "complete irresponsibility and lie and misstate facts and have no one to be answerable to", and "the preachers, liberals and professors" who had turned against him.[[260]](#cite_note-262)

In the [congressional elections of 1966](/wiki/1966_United_States_elections "1966 United States elections"), the Republicans gained three seats in the Senate and 47 in the House, reinvigorating the [conservative coalition](/wiki/Conservative_coalition "Conservative coalition"), which made it more difficult for Johnson to pass additional Great Society legislation. However, Congress ultimately passed almost 96 percent of the administration's Great Society programs.[[261]](#cite_note-263)

### Space program

Further information: [Space Race](/wiki/Space_Race "Space Race") and [Space policy of the United States](/wiki/Space_policy_of_the_United_States "Space policy of the United States")

[![President Johnson and Vice President Spiro Agnew witnessing the liftoff of Apollo 11.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Spiro_Agnew_and_Lyndon_Johnson_Watch_the_Apollo_11_Liftoff_-_GPN-2002-000068.jpg/250px-Spiro_Agnew_and_Lyndon_Johnson_Watch_the_Apollo_11_Liftoff_-_GPN-2002-000068.jpg)](/wiki/File:Spiro_Agnew_and_Lyndon_Johnson_Watch_the_Apollo_11_Liftoff_-_GPN-2002-000068.jpg)

Former President Johnson (center left) and Vice President [Spiro Agnew](/wiki/Spiro_Agnew "Spiro Agnew") (center right) witness the liftoff of [Apollo 11](/wiki/Apollo_11 "Apollo 11"), the first manned space aircraft to [land on the Moon](/wiki/Moon_landing "Moon landing"), on July 16, 1969

During the Johnson administration, [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA") conducted the [Gemini](/wiki/Project_Gemini "Project Gemini") crewed space program, developed the [Saturn V](/wiki/Saturn_V "Saturn V") rocket and [its launch facility](/wiki/Kennedy_Space_Center_Launch_Complex_39 "Kennedy Space Center Launch Complex 39"), and prepared to make the first crewed [Apollo program](/wiki/Apollo_program "Apollo program") flights. On January 27, 1967, the nation was stunned when the entire crew of [Apollo 1](/wiki/Apollo_1 "Apollo 1") was killed in a cabin fire during a spacecraft test on the launch pad, stopping Apollo in its tracks. Rather than appointing another Warren-style commission, Johnson accepted Administrator [James E. Webb](/wiki/James_E._Webb "James E. Webb")'s request for NASA to do its own investigation.[[262]](#cite_note-264)

Johnson maintained his staunch support of Apollo through Congressional and press controversy, and the program recovered. The first two crewed missions, [Apollo 7](/wiki/Apollo_7 "Apollo 7") and the first crewed flight to the Moon, [Apollo 8](/wiki/Apollo_8 "Apollo 8"), were completed by the end of Johnson's term. He congratulated the Apollo 8 crew, saying, "You've taken ... all of us, all over the world, into a new era."[[263]](#cite_note-265)[[264]](#cite_note-266) On July 16, 1969, Johnson attended the launch of the first Moon landing mission [Apollo 11](/wiki/Apollo_11 "Apollo 11"), becoming the first former or incumbent U.S. president to witness a rocket launch.[[265]](#cite_note-267)

### Vietnam War

Main article: [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War")

Further information: [United States in the Vietnam War](/wiki/United_States_in_the_Vietnam_War "United States in the Vietnam War") and [Presidency of Lyndon B. Johnson § Vietnam](/wiki/Presidency_of_Lyndon_B._Johnson#Vietnam "Presidency of Lyndon B. Johnson")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Robert_S._McNamara_and_General_Westmoreland_in_Vietnam_1965.png/250px-Robert_S._McNamara_and_General_Westmoreland_in_Vietnam_1965.png)](/wiki/File:Robert_S._McNamara_and_General_Westmoreland_in_Vietnam_1965.png)

Secretary of Defense [Robert McNamara](/wiki/Robert_McNamara "Robert McNamara") and [General Westmoreland](/wiki/William_Westmoreland "William Westmoreland") in [Da Nang](/wiki/Da_Nang "Da Nang") in August 1965

The [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War") began in 1955 as Communist forces started operating in [South Vietnam](/wiki/South_Vietnam "South Vietnam"). By the time Johnson took office, there were 16,700 American military personnel in South Vietnam.[[266]](#cite_note-268) Despite some misgivings, Johnson ultimately came to support escalation of the U.S. role in Vietnam.[[267]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson1996600–601-269) Like the vast majority of American leaders in the mid-1960s, he was determined to prevent the spread of Communism.[[268]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson1996604–605-270) Johnson's decision to escalate was also influenced heavily by reputation. Under pressure from pro-war politicians like [Barry Goldwater](/wiki/Barry_Goldwater "Barry Goldwater"), Johnson feared that if he made the decision to not stand firm in Vietnam he would lose domestic political credibility as well as contribute to a decline in the international reputation of the U.S.[[269]](#cite_note-271)
On October 11, 1963, President Kennedy had signed [NSAM 263](/wiki/National_Security_Action_Memorandum_263 "National Security Action Memorandum 263") ordering the withdrawal of 1,000 military personnel by the end of the year following recommendations of the [McNamara–Taylor mission](/wiki/McNamara%E2%80%93Taylor_mission "McNamara–Taylor mission") report.[[270]](#cite_note-272)[[271]](#cite_note-273)[[272]](#cite_note-McNamara-Taylor-274) On November 26, Johnson signed [NSAM 273](/wiki/National_Security_Action_Memorandum_273 "National Security Action Memorandum 273") which reaffirmed the Kennedy administration withdrawal approval and continued support for South Vietnam.[[273]](#cite_note-275)[[274]](#cite_note-276) In August 1964, ambiguous evidence suggested two U.S. destroyers had been [attacked](/wiki/Gulf_of_Tonkin_incident "Gulf of Tonkin incident") by North Vietnamese torpedo boats in international waters in the [Gulf of Tonkin](/wiki/Gulf_of_Tonkin "Gulf of Tonkin"). Although Johnson very much wanted to keep discussions about Vietnam out of the 1964 election campaign, he felt forced to respond to the supposed Communist aggression. He obtained from Congress the [Gulf of Tonkin Resolution](/wiki/Gulf_of_Tonkin_Resolution "Gulf of Tonkin Resolution") on August 7, 1964, giving blanket congressional approval for use of military force to repel future attacks.[[275]](#cite_note-277)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Boeing_B-52_dropping_bombs.jpg/250px-Boeing_B-52_dropping_bombs.jpg)](/wiki/File:Boeing_B-52_dropping_bombs.jpg)

Tens of thousands of Vietnamese civilians were [killed](/wiki/Vietnam_War_casualties "Vietnam War casualties") during the bombing of North Vietnam in [Operation Rolling Thunder](/wiki/Operation_Rolling_Thunder "Operation Rolling Thunder").[[276]](#cite_note-278)

Johnson decided on a systematic bombing campaign, which became known as [Operation Rolling Thunder](/wiki/Operation_Rolling_Thunder "Operation Rolling Thunder"), in February 1965 after an attack by [Viet Cong](/wiki/Viet_Cong "Viet Cong") guerrillas on [Pleiku Air Base](/wiki/Pleiku_Air_Base "Pleiku Air Base"), killing eight Americans.[[277]](#cite_note-279) The U.S. would continue to bomb North Vietnam until late 1968.[[278]](#cite_note-280) In March 1965, McGeorge Bundy called for American ground operations; Johnson agreed and also quietly changed the mission from defensive to offensive operations.[[279]](#cite_note-281) On March 8, 1965, 3,500 troops went ashore near [Da Nang](/wiki/Da_Nang "Da Nang"), the first time U.S. combat forces had been sent to mainland Asia since the [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War").[[280]](#cite_note-282) In June, South Vietnamese Ambassador [Maxwell D. Taylor](/wiki/Maxwell_D._Taylor "Maxwell D. Taylor") reported that the bombing offensive against North Vietnam had been ineffective and that the [South Vietnamese army](/wiki/Army_of_the_Republic_of_Vietnam "Army of the Republic of Vietnam") was outclassed and in danger of collapse.[[281]](#cite_note-283) In late July, McNamara and Johnson's top advisors recommended an increase in U.S. soldiers from 75,000 to over 200,000.[[282]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson1996612–613-284) Johnson agreed but felt boxed in by unpalatable choices. If he sent additional troops he would be attacked as an interventionist, and if he did not, he thought he risked being impeached.[[283]](#cite_note-dallek272277-285) By October 1965, there were over 200,000 troops deployed in Vietnam.[[284]](#cite_note-286) Throughout 1965, few members of Congress or the administration openly criticized Johnson's handling of the war.[[285]](#cite_note-287)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/OCTOBER_1966_-_PRESIDENT_JOHNSON_VISITS_U.S._SOLDIERS_AT_CAM_RANH_BAY_IN_SOUTH_VIETNAM.jpg/250px-OCTOBER_1966_-_PRESIDENT_JOHNSON_VISITS_U.S._SOLDIERS_AT_CAM_RANH_BAY_IN_SOUTH_VIETNAM.jpg)](/wiki/File:OCTOBER_1966_-_PRESIDENT_JOHNSON_VISITS_U.S._SOLDIERS_AT_CAM_RANH_BAY_IN_SOUTH_VIETNAM.jpg)

President Johnson shakes hands with U.S. airmen at [Cam Ranh Bay](/wiki/Cam_Ranh_Bay "Cam Ranh Bay") in South Vietnam, c. October 1966.

In early 1966, Senator Robert F. Kennedy harshly criticized Johnson's bombing campaign, stating that the U.S. may be headed "on a road from which there is no turning back, a road that leads to catastrophe for all mankind."[[286]](#cite_note-VietnamAPP-288) Soon thereafter, the [Senate Foreign Relations Committee](/wiki/United_States_Senate_Committee_on_Foreign_Relations "United States Senate Committee on Foreign Relations"), chaired by Senator [James William Fulbright](/wiki/James_William_Fulbright "James William Fulbright"), held televised hearings examining the administration's Vietnam policy.[[287]](#cite_note-289) In July, polling results indicated that Americans favored the bombing campaign by a five-to-one margin; however, in August a [Defense Department](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Defense "United States Department of Defense") study indicated that the bombing campaign was having minimal impact on North Vietnam.[[288]](#cite_note-290) By late 1966, multiple sources began to report progress was being made against the North Vietnamese logistics and infrastructure; Johnson was urged to begin peace discussions. English philosopher Bertrand Russell initiated the [International War Crimes Tribunal](/wiki/Russell_Tribunal "Russell Tribunal") to condemn the American effort.[[289]](#cite_note-291) The gap with Hanoi, however, was an unbridgeable demand on both sides for a unilateral end to bombing and withdrawal of forces.[[290]](#cite_note-292) Johnson grew more and more anxious about justifying war casualties, and talked of the need for decisive victory, despite the unpopularity of the cause.[[291]](#cite_note-293) By the end of 1966, it was clear that the air campaign and the pacification effort had both failed, and Johnson agreed to McNamara's new recommendation to add 70,000 troops in 1967 and the CIA's recommendations to increased bombings against North Vietnam.[[292]](#cite_note-294) The bombing escalation ended secret talks being held with North Vietnam, but U.S. leaders did not consider North Vietnamese intentions in those talks to be genuine.[[293]](#cite_note-295)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/A_female_demonstrator_offers_a_flower_to_military_police_on_guard_at_the_Pentagon_during_an_anti-Vietnam_demonstration._Arlington%2C_Virginia%2C_USA.jpg/250px-A_female_demonstrator_offers_a_flower_to_military_police_on_guard_at_the_Pentagon_during_an_anti-Vietnam_demonstration._Arlington%2C_Virginia%2C_USA.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_female_demonstrator_offers_a_flower_to_military_police_on_guard_at_the_Pentagon_during_an_anti-Vietnam_demonstration._Arlington,_Virginia,_USA.jpg)

A female demonstrator offers a flower to a soldier during the [1967 March on the Pentagon](/wiki/1967_March_on_the_Pentagon "1967 March on the Pentagon") anti-Vietnam War protest.

By the middle of 1967 nearly 70,000 Americans had been killed or wounded in the war, which was being commonly described in the news media and elsewhere as a "stalemate."[[294]](#cite_note-296) In January and February, probes were made to assess North Vietnamese's willingness to discuss peace, but they fell on deaf ears. [Ho Chi Minh](/wiki/Ho_Chi_Minh "Ho Chi Minh") declared that the only solution was a unilateral U.S. withdrawal.[[295]](#cite_note-297) A [Gallup, Inc.](/wiki/Gallup,_Inc. "Gallup, Inc.") poll in July 1967 showed that 52 percent of Americans disapproved of the president's handling of the war, and only 34 percent thought progress was being made.[[296]](#cite_note-298) Nonetheless, Johnson agreed to an increase of 55,000 troops, bringing the total to 525,000.[[297]](#cite_note-dallek473-299) In August, Johnson, with the Joint Chiefs' support, decided to expand the air campaign and exempted only Hanoi, Haiphong and a buffer zone with [China](/wiki/China "China") from the target list.[[298]](#cite_note-300) Later that month McNamara told a Senate subcommittee that an expanded air campaign would not bring Hanoi to the peace table. The Joint Chiefs were astounded, and threatened mass resignation; McNamara was summoned to the White House for a three-hour dressing down. Nevertheless, Johnson had received reports from the CIA confirming McNamara's analysis at least in part. In the meantime an election establishing a constitutional government in the South was concluded and provided hope for peace talks.[[299]](#cite_note-301)

With the war arguably in a stalemate and in light of the widespread disapproval of the conflict, Johnson convened a group of veteran government foreign policy experts, informally known as "the Wise Men": [Dean Acheson](/wiki/Dean_Acheson "Dean Acheson"), General [Omar Bradley](/wiki/Omar_Bradley "Omar Bradley"), [George Ball](/wiki/George_Ball_(diplomat) "George Ball (diplomat)"), [McGeorge Bundy](/wiki/McGeorge_Bundy "McGeorge Bundy"), [Arthur Dean](/wiki/Arthur_Dean_(lawyer) "Arthur Dean (lawyer)"), [C. Douglas Dillon](/wiki/C._Douglas_Dillon "C. Douglas Dillon"), [Abe Fortas](/wiki/Abe_Fortas "Abe Fortas"), [Averell Harriman](/wiki/Averell_Harriman "Averell Harriman"), [Henry Cabot Lodge](/wiki/Henry_Cabot_Lodge_Jr. "Henry Cabot Lodge Jr."), [Robert Daniel Murphy](/wiki/Robert_Daniel_Murphy "Robert Daniel Murphy"), and [Maxwell D. Taylor](/wiki/Maxwell_D._Taylor "Maxwell D. Taylor").[[300]](#cite_note-302) They unanimously opposed leaving Vietnam, and encouraged Johnson to "stay the course."[[301]](#cite_note-PoliticoWiseMen-303) Afterward, on November 17, in a nationally televised address, the president assured the American public, "We are inflicting greater losses than we're taking...We are making progress." Less than two weeks later, Robert McNamara announced his resignation as Defense Secretary. Behind closed doors, he had begun regularly expressing doubts over Johnson's war strategy, angering the president. He joined a growing list of Johnson's top aides who resigned over the war, including Bill Moyers, McGeorge Bundy, and George Ball.[[286]](#cite_note-VietnamAPP-288)[[302]](#cite_note-304) In October, with ever-increasing [public protests against the war](/wiki/Opposition_to_United_States_involvement_in_the_Vietnam_War "Opposition to United States involvement in the Vietnam War"), Johnson engaged the FBI and the CIA to investigate, monitor, and undermine anti-war activists.[[303]](#cite_note-305) In mid-October, there was a demonstration of 100,000 at [the Pentagon](/wiki/The_Pentagon "The Pentagon"); Johnson and [Dean Rusk](/wiki/Dean_Rusk "Dean Rusk") were convinced that foreign communist sources were behind the demonstration, but that was refuted in the CIA's findings.[[304]](#cite_note-306)

#### Tet Offensive

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/L_B_Johnson_Model_Khe_Sanh.jpeg/250px-L_B_Johnson_Model_Khe_Sanh.jpeg)](/wiki/File:L_B_Johnson_Model_Khe_Sanh.jpeg)

[Walt Rostow](/wiki/Walt_Rostow "Walt Rostow"), Johnson's [national security advisor](/wiki/National_Security_Advisor_(United_States) "National Security Advisor (United States)"), meeting with Johnson in the [Situation Room](/wiki/Situation_Room "Situation Room") in 1968, where the two reviewed a map of the region where the [Battle of Khe Sanh](/wiki/Battle_of_Khe_Sanh "Battle of Khe Sanh") was being waged

On January 30, 1968, the Viet Cong and the [North Vietnamese Army](/wiki/North_Vietnamese_Army "North Vietnamese Army") began the [Tet Offensive](/wiki/Tet_Offensive "Tet Offensive") against South Vietnam's five largest cities, including Saigon. While the Tet Offensive failed militarily, it was a psychological victory, definitively turning American public opinion against the war effort. In February 1968, influential news anchor [Walter Cronkite](/wiki/Walter_Cronkite "Walter Cronkite") of [CBS News](/wiki/CBS_News "CBS News") expressed on the air that the conflict was deadlocked and that additional fighting would change nothing. Johnson reacted, saying "If I've lost Cronkite, I've lost middle America".[[305]](#cite_note-307) Indeed, demoralization about the war was everywhere; 26 percent then approved of Johnson's handling of Vietnam, while 63 percent disapproved.[[306]](#cite_note-308) College students and others protested, burned [draft](/wiki/Conscription_in_the_United_States "Conscription in the United States") cards, and chanted, "Hey, hey, LBJ, how many kids did you kill today?"[[307]](#cite_note-309)

The Tet Offensive convinced senior leaders of the Johnson administration, including the "Wise Men" and new Defense Secretary [Clark Clifford](/wiki/Clark_Clifford "Clark Clifford"), that further escalation of troop levels would not help bring an end to the war.[[308]](#cite_note-310) Johnson was initially reluctant to follow this advice, but ultimately agreed to allow a partial bombing halt and to signal his willingness to engage in peace talks.[[309]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson1996683–684-311) On March 31, 1968, Johnson announced that he would halt the bombing in North Vietnam, while at the same time announcing that he would not seek re-election.[[310]](#cite_note-312) He also escalated U.S. military operations in South Vietnam in order to consolidate control of as much of the countryside as possible before the onset of serious peace talks.[[311]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson1996684–685-313) Talks began in Paris in May, but failed to yield any results.[[312]](#cite_note-314) Two of the major obstacles in negotiations were the unwillingness of the United States to allow the Viet Cong to take part in the South Vietnamese government, and the unwillingness of North Vietnam to recognize the legitimacy of South Vietnam.[[313]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson1996703-315) In October 1968, when the parties came close to an agreement on a bombing halt, Republican presidential nominee Richard Nixon intervened with the South Vietnamese, promising better terms so as to delay a settlement on the issue until after the election.[[314]](#cite_note-316) Johnson sought a continuation of talks after the 1968 election, but the North Vietnamese argued about procedural matters until after Nixon took office.[[315]](#cite_note-317)

### Dominican Republic

Like President Kennedy, Johnson sought to isolate [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba"), which was under the rule of the Soviet-aligned [Fidel Castro](/wiki/Fidel_Castro "Fidel Castro").[[316]](#cite_note-318)

In 1965, the [Dominican Civil War](/wiki/Dominican_Civil_War "Dominican Civil War") broke out between the government of President [Donald Reid Cabral](/wiki/Donald_Reid_Cabral "Donald Reid Cabral") and supporters of former president [Juan Bosch](/wiki/Juan_Bosch_(politician) "Juan Bosch (politician)").[[317]](#cite_note-herring732736-319) On the advice of Abe Fortas, Johnson dispatched over 20,000 Marines to the Dominican Republic.[[318]](#cite_note-320) Their role was not take sides but to evacuate American citizens and restore order. The U.S. also helped arrange an agreement providing for new elections. Johnson's use of force in ending the civil war alienated many in Latin America, and the region's importance to the administration receded as Johnson's foreign policy became increasingly dominated by the Vietnam War.[[317]](#cite_note-herring732736-319)

### Soviet Union

Main article: [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Glassboro-meeting1967.jpg/250px-Glassboro-meeting1967.jpg)](/wiki/File:Glassboro-meeting1967.jpg)

Soviet Premier [Alexei Kosygin](/wiki/Alexei_Kosygin "Alexei Kosygin") (left) next to Johnson during the [Glassboro Summit Conference](/wiki/Glassboro_Summit_Conference "Glassboro Summit Conference")

Though actively engaged in containment in Southeast Asia, the Middle East and Latin America, Johnson made it a priority to seek arms control deals with Moscow.[[319]](#cite_note-brands1-321) The Soviet Union also sought closer relations to the United States during the mid-to-late 1960s, partly due to the increasingly worse [Sino-Soviet split](/wiki/Sino-Soviet_split "Sino-Soviet split"). Johnson attempted to reduce tensions with [China](/wiki/China "China") by easing restrictions on trade, but the beginning of China's [Cultural Revolution](/wiki/Cultural_Revolution "Cultural Revolution") ended hopes of a greater rapprochement.[[320]](#cite_note-322) Johnson was concerned with averting the possibility of nuclear war, and he sought to reduce tensions in Europe.[[321]](#cite_note-323) The Johnson administration pursued arms control agreements with the Soviet Union, signing the [Outer Space Treaty](/wiki/Outer_Space_Treaty "Outer Space Treaty") and the [Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons](/wiki/Treaty_on_the_Non-Proliferation_of_Nuclear_Weapons "Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons"), and laid the foundation for the [Strategic Arms Limitation Talks](/wiki/Strategic_Arms_Limitation_Talks "Strategic Arms Limitation Talks").[[319]](#cite_note-brands1-321) Johnson held a largely amicable meeting with Soviet Premier [Alexei Kosygin](/wiki/Alexei_Kosygin "Alexei Kosygin") at the [Glassboro Summit Conference](/wiki/Glassboro_Summit_Conference "Glassboro Summit Conference") in 1967, and in July 1968 the United States, Britain, and the Soviet Union signed the Non-Proliferation Treaty, in which each signatory agreed not to help other countries develop or acquire nuclear weapons. A planned nuclear disarmament summit between the United States and the Soviet Union was scuttled after Soviet forces [violently suppressed](/wiki/Warsaw_Pact_invasion_of_Czechoslovakia "Warsaw Pact invasion of Czechoslovakia") the [Prague Spring](/wiki/Prague_Spring "Prague Spring"), an attempted democratization of [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia").[[322]](#cite_note-324)

### Surveillance of Martin Luther King

Johnson continued the FBI's [wiretapping of Martin Luther King Jr.](/wiki/Wiretapping_of_Martin_Luther_King_Jr. "Wiretapping of Martin Luther King Jr.") authorized by the Kennedy administration under Attorney General Robert F. Kennedy.[[323]](#cite_note-the_atlantic-325) Johnson also authorized the tapping of phone conversations of others, including the Vietnamese friends of a Nixon associate.[[324]](#cite_note-326)

### International trips

Further information: [List of international trips made by the President of the United States § Lyndon B. Johnson](/wiki/List_of_international_trips_made_by_the_President_of_the_United_States#Lyndon_B._Johnson "List of international trips made by the President of the United States")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/US_President_Lyndon_Johnson_Presidential_Trips.PNG/500px-US_President_Lyndon_Johnson_Presidential_Trips.PNG)](/wiki/File:US_President_Lyndon_Johnson_Presidential_Trips.PNG)

Countries visited by Johnson during his presidency

Johnson made eleven international trips to twenty countries during his presidency.[[325]](#cite_note-327) He flew five hundred twenty-three thousand miles (841,690 km) aboard [Air Force One](/wiki/Air_Force_One "Air Force One") while in office. His October 1966 visit to Australia sparked demonstrations from anti-war protesters.[[326]](#cite_note-328) One of the most unusual international trips in presidential history occurred before Christmas 1967. The President began the trip by going to the memorial for Australian Prime Minister [Harold Holt](/wiki/Harold_Holt "Harold Holt"), who was presumed drowned in [a swimming accident](/wiki/Disappearance_of_Harold_Holt "Disappearance of Harold Holt"). The White House did not reveal in advance to the press that the President would make the first round-the-world presidential trip. The trip was twenty-six thousand nine hundred fifty-nine miles (43,386.3 km) completed in only 112.5 hours (4.7 days). Air Force One crossed the equator twice, stopped at [Travis Air Force Base](/wiki/Travis_Air_Force_Base "Travis Air Force Base"), in [Honolulu](/wiki/Honolulu "Honolulu"), [Pago Pago](/wiki/Pago_Pago "Pago Pago"), [Canberra](/wiki/Canberra "Canberra"), [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne"), [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam"), [Karachi](/wiki/Karachi "Karachi"), and [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome").[[327]](#cite_note-329)

### 1968 presidential election

Main article: [1968 United States presidential election](/wiki/1968_United_States_presidential_election "1968 United States presidential election")

See also: [Dump Johnson movement](/wiki/Dump_Johnson_movement "Dump Johnson movement")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Lyndon_Johnson_Richard_Nixon_1968.jpg/250px-Lyndon_Johnson_Richard_Nixon_1968.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lyndon_Johnson_Richard_Nixon_1968.jpg)

Johnson meeting with Republican presidential candidate [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon") in the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House") in July 1968

As he had served less than two years of President Kennedy's term, Johnson was [constitutionally eligible](/wiki/Twenty-second_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Twenty-second Amendment to the United States Constitution") for election to a second full term in the 1968 presidential election.[[328]](#cite_note-330)[[329]](#cite_note-331) Despite Johnson's growing unpopularity, conventional wisdom held that it would be impossible to deny re-nomination to a sitting president.[[330]](#cite_note-332) In September 1967 Johnson openly admitted he was considering dropping out of the race for re-election.[[331]](#cite_note-333) Johnson won a narrow victory in the [New Hampshire presidential primary](/wiki/1968_New_Hampshire_Democratic_presidential_primary "1968 New Hampshire Democratic presidential primary") on March 12,[[332]](#cite_note-334) but in a March 31 speech, Johnson shocked the nation when he announced he would not run for re-election by concluding with the line: ["I shall not seek, and I will not accept, the nomination of my party for another term as your president"](/wiki/Withdrawal_of_Lyndon_B._Johnson_from_the_1968_United_States_presidential_election "Withdrawal of Lyndon B. Johnson from the 1968 United States presidential election").[[333]](#cite_note-335) The next day, his approval ratings increased from 36 percent to 49 percent.[[334]](#cite_note-336)

Historians have debated the factors that led to Johnson's surprise decision. Shesol says Johnson wanted out of the White House but also wanted vindication; when the indicators turned negative he decided to leave.[[335]](#cite_note-337) Woods writes that Johnson realized he needed to leave in order for the nation to heal.[[336]](#cite_note-338) Dallek says that Johnson had no further domestic goals, and realized that his personality had eroded his popularity. His health was not good, and he was preoccupied with the Kennedy campaign; his wife was pressing for his retirement and his base of support continued to shrink. Leaving the race would allow him to pose as a peacemaker.[[337]](#cite_note-339) Bennett, however, says Johnson "had been forced out of a reelection race in 1968 by outrage over his policy in Southeast Asia."[[338]](#cite_note-340) Johnson may also have hoped that the convention would ultimately choose to draft him back into the race.[[339]](#cite_note-341)

Vice President [Hubert Humphrey](/wiki/Hubert_Humphrey "Hubert Humphrey") entered the race after Johnson's withdrawal, making the 1968 Democratic primaries a three-way contest between Humphrey, Kennedy, and McCarthy. Kennedy cut into McCarthy's liberal and anti-war base, while also winning the support of the poor and working class. He won a series of primary victories, but was [assassinated](/wiki/Robert_Kennedy%27s_assassination "Robert Kennedy's assassination") in June by [Sirhan Sirhan](/wiki/Sirhan_Sirhan "Sirhan Sirhan"), an [Arab](/wiki/Arabs "Arabs") nationalist.[[340]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson1996691–693-342) With Johnson's support, Humphrey won the presidential nomination at the tumultuous [1968 Democratic National Convention](/wiki/1968_Democratic_National_Convention "1968 Democratic National Convention"), held in [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") in late August. Violent police attacks against [anti-war protesters](/wiki/1968_Democratic_National_Convention_protest_activity "1968 Democratic National Convention protest activity") in Chicago marred the convention.[[341]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPatterson1996694–697-343)

Humphrey's polling numbers improved after a September 30 speech in which he broke with Johnson's war policy, calling for an end to the bombing of North Vietnam.[[342]](#cite_note-sabato1-344) In what was termed the [October surprise](/wiki/October_surprise "October surprise"), Johnson announced to the nation on October 31, 1968, that he had ordered a complete cessation of "all air, naval and artillery bombardment of North Vietnam", effective November 1, should the North Vietnamese government be willing to negotiate and citing progress with the [Paris peace talks](/wiki/Paris_Peace_Accords "Paris Peace Accords").[[343]](#cite_note-345)

Republican nominee [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon") won the election.[[342]](#cite_note-sabato1-344)

### Judicial appointments

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/Thurgood_Marshall_and_President_Lyndon_B._Johnson_June_13%2C_1967_-_LBJ_Museum_C5706-1_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Thurgood_Marshall_and_President_Lyndon_B._Johnson_June_13%2C_1967_-_LBJ_Museum_C5706-1_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thurgood_Marshall_and_President_Lyndon_B._Johnson_June_13,_1967_-_LBJ_Museum_C5706-1_(cropped).jpg)

Appointed by Johnson in 1967, [Thurgood Marshall](/wiki/Thurgood_Marshall "Thurgood Marshall") (left) became the first African American on the [U.S. Supreme Court](/wiki/U.S._Supreme_Court "U.S. Supreme Court")

See also: [Lyndon B. Johnson judicial appointments](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson_judicial_appointments "Lyndon B. Johnson judicial appointments") and [Lyndon B. Johnson judicial appointment controversies](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson_judicial_appointment_controversies "Lyndon B. Johnson judicial appointment controversies")

Johnson appointed Justices [Abe Fortas](/wiki/Abe_Fortas "Abe Fortas") (1965) and [Thurgood Marshall](/wiki/Thurgood_Marshall "Thurgood Marshall") (1967) to the [Supreme Court of the United States](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States"). Johnson anticipated court challenges to his legislative measures in 1965 and thought it advantageous to have a "mole" in the Supreme Court to provide him with inside information, as he was able to get from the legislative branch. Abe Fortas in particular Johnson thought could fill the bill. The opportunity arose when an opening occurred for ambassador to the UN, with Adlai Stevenson's death; Associate Justice [Arthur Goldberg](/wiki/Arthur_Goldberg "Arthur Goldberg") accepted Johnson's offer to transfer to the UN position. Johnson insisted on Fortas assuming Goldberg's seat, over Fortas's wife's objection that it was too early in his career.[[344]](#cite_note-346) When [Earl Warren](/wiki/Earl_Warren "Earl Warren") announced his retirement in 1968, Johnson nominated Fortas to succeed him as [Chief Justice of the United States](/wiki/Chief_Justice_of_the_United_States "Chief Justice of the United States"), and nominated [Homer Thornberry](/wiki/Homer_Thornberry "Homer Thornberry") to succeed Fortas as associate justice. However, Fortas's nomination was filibustered by senators, and neither nominee was voted upon by the full Senate.[[345]](#cite_note-347)

## Post-presidency (1969–1973)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Portrait_of_President_Lyndon_B._Johnson_%28D3007-16%29.jpg/250px-Portrait_of_President_Lyndon_B._Johnson_%28D3007-16%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portrait_of_President_Lyndon_B._Johnson_(D3007-16).jpg)

Portrait of Johnson taken nine days before leaving office in 1969.

> On Inauguration Day (January 20, 1969), Johnson saw Nixon sworn in, then got on the plane to fly back to Texas. When the front door of the plane closed, Johnson pulled out a cigarette ‍—‌ his first cigarette he had smoked since his heart attack in 1955. One of his daughters pulled it out of his mouth and said, "Daddy, what are you doing? You're going to kill yourself." He took it back and said, "I've now raised you, girls. I've now been President. *Now it's my time!*" From that point on, he went into a very self-destructive spiral.

— Historian [Michael Beschloss](/wiki/Michael_Beschloss "Michael Beschloss")[[346]](#cite_note-348)

After leaving the presidency in January 1969, Johnson went home to his ranch in Stonewall, Texas, accompanied by former aide and speechwriter [Harry J. Middleton](/wiki/Harry_J._Middleton "Harry J. Middleton"), who would draft Johnson's first book, *The Choices We Face,* and work with him on his memoirs, *The Vantage Point: Perspectives of the Presidency 1963–1969,* published in 1971.[[347]](#cite_note-349) That year, the [Lyndon Baines Johnson Library and Museum](/wiki/Lyndon_Baines_Johnson_Library_and_Museum "Lyndon Baines Johnson Library and Museum") opened on the campus of [The University of Texas at Austin](/wiki/University_of_Texas_at_Austin "University of Texas at Austin"). He donated his Texas ranch in his will to the public to form the [Lyndon B. Johnson National Historical Park](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson_National_Historical_Park "Lyndon B. Johnson National Historical Park"), with the provision that it "remain a working ranch and not become a sterile relic of the past".[[348]](#cite_note-350)

Johnson gave Nixon high grades in foreign policy, but worried that his successor was being pressured into removing U.S. forces from South Vietnam before the South Vietnamese were able to defend themselves. "If the South falls to the Communists, we can have a serious backlash here at home," he warned.[[141]](#cite_note-LastDays-142)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Lyndon_B._Johnson_1972.jpg/250px-Lyndon_B._Johnson_1972.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lyndon_B._Johnson_1972.jpg)

Johnson with longer hair during an interview in August 1972, five months before his death

During the [1972 presidential election](/wiki/1972_United_States_presidential_election "1972 United States presidential election"), Johnson only reluctantly endorsed Democratic nominee [George McGovern](/wiki/George_McGovern "George McGovern"), a senator from [South Dakota](/wiki/South_Dakota "South Dakota"); McGovern had long opposed Johnson's foreign and defense policies. Johnson wanted to attend the Democratic National Convention, but was advised not to attend as he would not be welcome. The McGovern nomination and platform dismayed him. Nixon could be defeated, Johnson insisted, "if only the Democrats don't go too far left".[[141]](#cite_note-LastDays-142) Johnson felt [Edmund Muskie](/wiki/Edmund_Muskie "Edmund Muskie") would be more likely to defeat Nixon; however, he declined to try to stop McGovern receiving the nomination as he felt his unpopularity within the Democratic Party was such that anything he said was more likely to help McGovern. Johnson's protégé [John Connally](/wiki/John_Connally "John Connally") had served as President Nixon's Secretary of the Treasury and then stepped down to head "[Democrats for Nixon](/wiki/Democrats_for_Nixon "Democrats for Nixon")", a group funded by Republicans. It was the first time that Connally and Johnson were on opposite sides of a general election campaign.[[349]](#cite_note-351)

## Personal life

### Marriage and children

On November 17, 1934, Johnson married [Claudia Alta "Lady Bird" Taylor](/wiki/Lady_Bird_Johnson "Lady Bird Johnson") from [Karnack, Texas](/wiki/Karnack,_Texas "Karnack, Texas"). The two first met after he attended [Georgetown University Law Center](/wiki/Georgetown_University_Law_Center "Georgetown University Law Center") in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") for one semester.[[350]](#cite_note-352) During their first date, Johnson asked her to marry him; many dates later, she finally agreed.[[351]](#cite_note-353) The wedding was officiated by [Arthur R. McKinstry](/wiki/Arthur_R._McKinstry "Arthur R. McKinstry") at [St. Mark's Episcopal Church](/wiki/St._Mark%27s_Episcopal_Church_(San_Antonio,_Texas) "St. Mark's Episcopal Church (San Antonio, Texas)") in [San Antonio](/wiki/San_Antonio "San Antonio").[[352]](#cite_note-354) They had two daughters: [Lynda Bird](/wiki/Lynda_Bird_Johnson "Lynda Bird Johnson") in 1944 and [Luci Baines](/wiki/Luci_Baines_Johnson "Luci Baines Johnson") in 1947. Johnson gave his children names with the LBJ initials; his dog was named Little Beagle Johnson, and his home was the [LBJ Ranch](/wiki/LBJ_Ranch "LBJ Ranch") in the [Texas Hill Country](/wiki/Texas_Hill_Country "Texas Hill Country"). His initials were on his cufflinks, ashtrays, and clothes.[[353]](#cite_note-Inc1956-355) During his marriage, Johnson had [affairs](/wiki/Infidelity "Infidelity") with "numerous"[[354]](#cite_note-caro_2019-356) women, including socialite [Alice Marsh](/wiki/Alice_Marsh "Alice Marsh"), who was considered, outside his marriage, his most important relationship.[[354]](#cite_note-caro_2019-356)

### Health

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/LBJ-Ranch-1972.jpg/250px-LBJ-Ranch-1972.jpg)](/wiki/File:LBJ-Ranch-1972.jpg)

Johnson at his [Texas ranch](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson_National_Historical_Park "Lyndon B. Johnson National Historical Park"), 1972

On July 2, 1955, at age 46, Johnson, a 60-cigarette-per-day smoker, suffered a near-fatal [heart attack](/wiki/Myocardial_infarction "Myocardial infarction"), which inspired him to discontinue smoking. Five months later, Johnson's doctors reported he had made "a most satisfactory recovery".[[355]](#cite_note-357)[[356]](#cite_note-358)

Johnson may have suffered a second heart attack following President Kennedy's assassination, but the diagnosis released to the public was that he had an [angina](/wiki/Angina "Angina") attack.[[357]](#cite_note-359)

On November 8, 1965, Johnson underwent surgery at [Bethesda Naval Hospital](/wiki/Bethesda_Naval_Hospital "Bethesda Naval Hospital") to remove his [gallbladder](/wiki/Gallbladder "Gallbladder") and a [kidney stone](/wiki/Kidney_stone "Kidney stone"). After the procedure, Johnson's doctors reported that the president had come through the surgery "beautifully as expected."[[358]](#cite_note-360) He was able to resume his duties the following day, and he met with reporters a couple of days later to reassure the nation that he was recovering well. Although Johnson was incapacitated during surgery, there was no transfer of presidential power to Vice President Humphrey.[[359]](#cite_note-361)[[360]](#cite_note-362)

In March 1970, Johnson suffered an attack of [angina](/wiki/Angina "Angina") and was taken to [Brooke Army General Hospital](/wiki/San_Antonio_Military_Medical_Center "San Antonio Military Medical Center") in [San Antonio](/wiki/San_Antonio "San Antonio"). He had gained more than 25 pounds (11 kg) since leaving the White House; he now weighed around 235 pounds (107 kg) and was urged to lose considerable weight. By the summer of 1970, again gripped by chronic chest pains, Johnson lost 15 pounds (6.8 kg) in less than a month on a crash water diet. He had also resumed smoking shortly before Christmas 1971, having not smoked since his near-fatal heart attack in July 1955.[[141]](#cite_note-LastDays-142)

In April 1972, Johnson had another major heart attack while visiting his daughter, Lynda, in Virginia. "I'm hurting real bad",[[141]](#cite_note-LastDays-142) he confided to friends. The chest pains returned nearly every afternoon—jolting pains that left him frightened and breathless. A portable [oxygen tank](/wiki/Oxygen_tank "Oxygen tank") was kept by his bed, and he periodically interrupted what he was doing to lie down and don the mask. He continued to smoke heavily and, although nominally on a [low-calorie](/wiki/Low-calorie_diet "Low-calorie diet"), [low-cholesterol diet](/wiki/Dietary_cholesterol "Dietary cholesterol"), kept to it only intermittently. Meanwhile, he began to experience severe abdominal pains, diagnosed as [diverticulosis](/wiki/Diverticulosis "Diverticulosis"). His heart condition rapidly worsened and surgery was recommended. Johnson flew to Houston to consult with heart specialist [Michael DeBakey](/wiki/Michael_DeBakey "Michael DeBakey"), where he learned his condition was terminal. DeBakey found that despite two of Johnson's coronary arteries being in urgent need of a [coronary bypass](/wiki/Coronary_bypass "Coronary bypass"), his heart was in such poor condition that he likely would have died during surgery.[[141]](#cite_note-LastDays-142)

## Death

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/President_Richard_Nixon_Laying_a_Wreath_at_the_Casket_of_Former_President_Lyndon_Baines_Johnson_during_the_Lying_in_State_Memorial_Ceremony_in_the_Capitol_Rotunda.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Richard_Nixon_Laying_a_Wreath_at_the_Casket_of_Former_President_Lyndon_Baines_Johnson_during_the_Lying_in_State_Memorial_Ceremony_in_the_Capitol_Rotunda.jpg)

President [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon") paying his last tributes to his predecessor, former president Johnson in 1973

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Lyndon_Baines_Johnson_grave_cropped.jpeg/250px-Lyndon_Baines_Johnson_grave_cropped.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Lyndon_Baines_Johnson_grave_cropped.jpeg)

Johnson's grave

Johnson recorded an hour-long television interview with newsman [Walter Cronkite](/wiki/Walter_Cronkite "Walter Cronkite") at his ranch on January 12, 1973, in which he discussed his legacy, particularly about the civil rights movement. He was still smoking heavily, and told Cronkite that it was better for his heart "to smoke than to be nervous".[[361]](#cite_note-363)

At approximately 3:50 P.M. [Central](/wiki/Central_Time_Zone "Central Time Zone") on January 22, 1973, Johnson suffered his final heart attack in his bedroom. He managed to telephone the Secret Service agents on the ranch, who found him still holding the telephone receiver, unconscious and "appear[ing] to be dead".[[362]](#cite_note-AP-364) They attempted resuscitation, and Johnson was airlifted in one of his planes to [San Antonio International Airport](/wiki/San_Antonio_International_Airport "San Antonio International Airport"), en route to [Brooke Army Medical Center](/wiki/Brooke_Army_Medical_Center "Brooke Army Medical Center"). However, [cardiologist](/wiki/Cardiologist "Cardiologist") and [Army colonel](/wiki/Army_colonel "Army colonel") George McGranahan pronounced him [dead on arrival](/wiki/Dead_on_arrival "Dead on arrival") at the airport at 4:33 P.M. Johnson was 64.[[362]](#cite_note-AP-364)

Shortly after the former president was pronounced dead, Johnson's press secretary [Tom Johnson](/wiki/Tom_Johnson_(journalist) "Tom Johnson (journalist)") (no relation) telephoned Cronkite to tell him. Cronkite was anchoring *[CBS Evening News](/wiki/CBS_Evening_News "CBS Evening News")* live at the moment Johnson reached him, which enabled him to report on President Johnson's death as he received direct information.[[363]](#cite_note-365) Nixon mentioned Johnson's death in a speech he gave the day after Johnson died, announcing the peace agreement to end the Vietnam War.[[364]](#cite_note-366)[[365]](#cite_note-367)

### Funeral

Johnson was honored with a [state funeral](/wiki/State_funerals_in_the_United_States "State funerals in the United States"). His body initially [lay in repose](/wiki/Lay_in_repose "Lay in repose") at the [LBJ Presidential Library](/wiki/Lyndon_Baines_Johnson_Library_and_Museum "Lyndon Baines Johnson Library and Museum") in Austin, Texas, before being flown to Washington, D.C.[[366]](#cite_note-Capitol-368) He [lay in state](/wiki/Lying_in_state#United_States "Lying in state") at the [U.S. Capitol](/wiki/United_States_Capitol "United States Capitol"), where [J. J. Pickle](/wiki/J._J._Pickle "J. J. Pickle") and former Secretary of State [Dean Rusk](/wiki/Dean_Rusk "Dean Rusk") delivered eulogies.[[367]](#cite_note-369)[[366]](#cite_note-Capitol-368)

The state funeral service took place on January 25, 1973, at the [National City Christian Church](/wiki/National_City_Christian_Church "National City Christian Church") in Washington, D.C., where Johnson had worshiped while president. The service was presided over by President [Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon") and attended by foreign dignitaries, including [Eisaku Satō](/wiki/Eisaku_Sat%C5%8D "Eisaku Satō"), who had served as prime minister of Japan during Johnson's presidency.[[368]](#cite_note-370) Eulogies were delivered by George Davis, the church's pastor, and [W. Marvin Watson](/wiki/W._Marvin_Watson "W. Marvin Watson"), Johnson's last [postmaster general](/wiki/United_States_Postmaster_General "United States Postmaster General") and a longtime adviser.[[369]](#cite_note-Funeral-371)

Later that day, Johnson was buried in his family's private cemetery in [Gillespie County, Texas](/wiki/Gillespie_County,_Texas "Gillespie County, Texas"), near the house in which he was born. Reverend [Billy Graham](/wiki/Billy_Graham "Billy Graham") officiated the burial service, and former Texas governor [John Connally](/wiki/John_Connally "John Connally") delivered a eulogy; several hundred people attended.[[369]](#cite_note-Funeral-371)[[370]](#cite_note-372)

The state funeral—the last for a president until [Richard Nixon's in 1994](/wiki/Death_and_state_funeral_of_Richard_Nixon "Death and state funeral of Richard Nixon")—occurred the same week as [Nixon's second inauguration](/wiki/Second_inauguration_of_Richard_Nixon "Second inauguration of Richard Nixon").[[371]](#cite_note-MDW-373) Because Johnson died two days after the inauguration,[[366]](#cite_note-Capitol-368)[[371]](#cite_note-MDW-373) some inauguration-related activities were canceled.[[371]](#cite_note-MDW-373)

## Personality and public image

According to biographer Randall Woods, Johnson posed in many different roles:

> "Johnson the Son of the Tenant Farmer, Johnson the Great Compromiser, Johnson the All-Knowing, Johnson the Humble, Johnson the Warrior, Johnson the Dove, Johnson the Romantic, Johnson the Hard-Headed Pragmatist, Johnson the Preserver of Traditions, Johnson the Crusader for Social Justice, Johnson the Magnanimous, Johnson the Vindictive or Johnson the Uncouth, LBJ the Hick, Lyndon the Satyr, and Johnson the Usurper".[[372]](#cite_note-374)

Johnson had his particular brand of persuasion, known as "The Johnson Treatment".[[373]](#cite_note-indy-375) "There was no more powerful majority leader in American history," biographer Robert Dallek writes. Dallek stated that Johnson had biographies on all the senators, knew what their ambitions, hopes, and tastes were and used it to his advantage in securing votes. Another Johnson biographer noted, "He could get up every day and learn what their fears, their desires, their wishes, their wants were and he could then manipulate, dominate, persuade and cajole them." As president, Johnson vetoed 30 bills; no other president in history vetoed so many bills and never had a single one overridden by Congress. He was often seen as an ambitious, tireless, and imposing figure who was ruthlessly effective at getting legislation passed. He typically worked 18- to 20-hour days without a break and had no regular leisure activities. He stood 6 feet 3.5 inches (1.918 m) tall.[[374]](#cite_note-376)[[375]](#cite_note-377)[[376]](#cite_note-378)

Johnson's cowboy hat and boots reflected his Texas roots and love of the rural hill country. From 250 acres (100 ha) of land that he was given by an aunt in 1951, he created a 2,700-acre (1,100 ha) working ranch with 400 cattle. The [National Park Service](/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service") keeps a herd descended from Johnson's and maintains the ranch property.[[377]](#cite_note-379)

Biographer Randall Woods argues that [Social Gospel](/wiki/Social_Gospel "Social Gospel") themes Johnson learned from childhood allowed him to transform social problems into moral problems. This helps explain his longtime commitment to social justice, and explicitly inspired his foreign-policy approach to Christian internationalism and nation-building. For example, in a 1966 speech he quoted at length from the [Social Creed of the Methodist Church](/wiki/Social_Creed_(Methodist) "Social Creed (Methodist)"), adding "It would be very hard for me to write a more perfect description of the American ideal."[[378]](#cite_note-380)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/LBJ_Library_2017.jpg/250px-LBJ_Library_2017.jpg)](/wiki/File:LBJ_Library_2017.jpg)

[Lyndon Baines Johnson Library and Museum](/wiki/Lyndon_Baines_Johnson_Library_and_Museum "Lyndon Baines Johnson Library and Museum") located on the campus of the [University of Texas](/wiki/University_of_Texas "University of Texas") in [Austin](/wiki/Austin,_Texas "Austin, Texas")

Scholars have viewed Johnson through the lens of both his legislative achievements and his lack of success in the Vietnam War. His overall rating among historians has remained relatively steady, and his average ranking is higher than any of the eight presidents who followed him, although similar to Reagan and Clinton.[[379]](#cite_note-381) In public polling of [presidential favorability](/wiki/Historical_rankings_of_presidents_of_the_United_States "Historical rankings of presidents of the United States") of Johnson and the presidents who succeeded him Johnson tends to appear more toward the bottom of lists, typically excepting [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump"), [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") and [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon"), and sometimes [Gerald Ford](/wiki/Gerald_Ford "Gerald Ford").

Historian Kent Germany explains:

> The man who was elected to the White House by one of the widest margins in U.S. history and pushed through as much legislation as any other American politician now seems to be remembered best by the public for succeeding an assassinated hero, steering the country into a quagmire in Vietnam, cheating on his saintly wife, exposing his stitched-up belly, using profanity, picking up dogs by their ears, swimming naked with advisers in the White House pool, and emptying his bowels while conducting official business. Of all those issues, Johnson's reputation suffers the most from his management of the Vietnam War, something that has overshadowed his civil rights and domestic policy accomplishments and caused Johnson himself to regret his handling of "the woman I really loved ‍—‌ the Great Society."[[380]](#cite_note-382)

### Memorials

See also: [List of memorials to Lyndon B. Johnson](/wiki/List_of_memorials_to_Lyndon_B._Johnson "List of memorials to Lyndon B. Johnson")

The Manned Spacecraft Center in [Houston](/wiki/Houston "Houston") was renamed the [Lyndon B. Johnson Space Center](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson_Space_Center "Lyndon B. Johnson Space Center") in 1973,[[381]](#cite_note-383) and the [United States Department of Education](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Education "United States Department of Education") headquarters was named after Johnson in 2007.[[382]](#cite_note-384) The [Lyndon B. Johnson School of Public Affairs](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson_School_of_Public_Affairs "Lyndon B. Johnson School of Public Affairs") at the University of Texas at Austin was named in his honor, as is the [Lyndon B. Johnson National Grassland](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson_National_Grassland "Lyndon B. Johnson National Grassland"). Also named for him are schools in Austin and [Laredo](/wiki/Laredo,_Texas "Laredo, Texas"), Texas; [Melbourne, Florida](/wiki/Melbourne,_Florida "Melbourne, Florida"); and [Jackson, Kentucky](/wiki/Jackson,_Kentucky "Jackson, Kentucky"). [Interstate 635](/wiki/Interstate_635_(Texas) "Interstate 635 (Texas)") in [Dallas](/wiki/Dallas "Dallas") is named the Lyndon B. Johnson Freeway. The [Lyndon Baines Johnson Memorial Grove on the Potomac](/wiki/Lyndon_Baines_Johnson_Memorial_Grove_on_the_Potomac "Lyndon Baines Johnson Memorial Grove on the Potomac") was dedicated in 1976.

Johnson was awarded the [Presidential Medal of Freedom](/wiki/Presidential_Medal_of_Freedom "Presidential Medal of Freedom") posthumously in 1980.[[383]](#cite_note-385) Texas created a state holiday on August 27 to mark Johnson's birthday, known as [Lyndon Baines Johnson Day](/wiki/Lyndon_Baines_Johnson_Day "Lyndon Baines Johnson Day").[[384]](#cite_note-386)

* 1964: [Urban Mass Transportation Act](/wiki/Urban_Mass_Transportation_Act_of_1964 "Urban Mass Transportation Act of 1964")
* 1964: [Wilderness Act](/wiki/Wilderness_Act "Wilderness Act")

* 1964: [Food Stamp Act](/wiki/Food_Stamp_Act_of_1964 "Food Stamp Act of 1964")
* 1964: [Economic Opportunity Act](/wiki/Economic_Opportunity_Act_of_1964 "Economic Opportunity Act of 1964")
* 1965: [Housing and Urban Development Act](/wiki/Housing_and_Urban_Development_Act_of_1965 "Housing and Urban Development Act of 1965")
* 1965: [Higher Education Act](/wiki/Higher_Education_Act_of_1965 "Higher Education Act of 1965")
* 1965: [Older Americans Act](/wiki/Older_Americans_Act "Older Americans Act")
* 1965: [Coinage Act](/wiki/Coinage_Act_of_1965 "Coinage Act of 1965")
* 1965: [Social Security Act](/wiki/Social_Security_Act_of_1965 "Social Security Act of 1965")
* 1965: [Voting Rights Act](/wiki/Voting_Rights_Act_of_1965 "Voting Rights Act of 1965")
* 1965: [Immigration and Nationality Services Act](/wiki/Immigration_and_Nationality_Services_Act_of_1965 "Immigration and Nationality Services Act of 1965")
* 1966: [Animal Welfare Act](/wiki/Animal_Welfare_Act_of_1966 "Animal Welfare Act of 1966")
* 1966: [Freedom of Information Act](/wiki/Freedom_of_Information_Act_(United_States) "Freedom of Information Act (United States)")
* 1967: [Age Discrimination in Employment Act](/wiki/Age_Discrimination_in_Employment_Act "Age Discrimination in Employment Act")[[390]](#cite_note-392)
* 1967: [Public Broadcasting Act](/wiki/Public_Broadcasting_Act_of_1967 "Public Broadcasting Act of 1967")
* 1968: [Architectural Barriers Act](/wiki/Architectural_Barriers_Act_of_1968 "Architectural Barriers Act of 1968")
* 1968: [Bilingual Education Act](/wiki/Bilingual_Education_Act "Bilingual Education Act")

* 1968: [Gun Control Act](/wiki/Gun_Control_Act_of_1968 "Gun Control Act of 1968")

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-1)** Johnson was vice president under John F. Kennedy and became president upon [Kennedy's assassination](/wiki/Assassination_of_John_F._Kennedy "Assassination of John F. Kennedy") on November 22, 1963. As this was prior to the adoption of the [Twenty-fifth Amendment](/wiki/Twenty-fifth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Twenty-fifth Amendment to the United States Constitution") in 1967, a vacancy in the office of vice president was not filled until the next ensuing election and inauguration.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-191)** President Grant, on October 17, 1871, suspended [habeas corpus](/wiki/Habeas_corpus "Habeas corpus") in nine [South Carolina](/wiki/South_Carolina "South Carolina") counties, sent in troops, and prosecuted the Klan in the federal district court.
